docs.title: Summary
docs.text: LakeHouse Query Pulse is a web-based application for managing data warehouse connections, executing queries, and analyzing query performance across Hive, Impala, and Trino environments. The application provides comprehensive configuration management with proper separation between administrative (global) settings and user-specific settings.

docs.title: System Administration
docs.text: This section covers database management, JVM configuration, LLM settings, and system monitoring.

docs.title: RocksDB Management
docs.text:
Scope: Global (Admin Only)

Manage the embedded RocksDB database used for all application data storage.

docs.title: Overview Tab
docs.text:
Health Status: - Database open/closed status - Read/write capability verification

Storage Statistics: - Total entries count - SST file count and size - Log file count and size

Column Families:

Family Purpose users User accounts workspaces Query workspaces queries Saved queries connections Database connections kerberos_configs Kerberos configurations tls_configs TLS configurations certificates SSL certificates keytabs Encrypted keytab files git_credentials Git authentication settings Application settings backups_metadata Backup tracking

docs.title: Data Browser Tab
docs.text:
Browse and inspect stored data:

  1. Select column family
  2. Optionally filter by key prefix
  3. Browse keys with pagination
  4. View values (JSON formatted if applicable)
  5. Copy values to clipboard
  6. Delete individual keys

docs.title: Maintenance Tab
docs.text:
Database Operations:

Operation Description Compact Merge SST files to reclaim space Flush Write memtables to disk

Backup Management:

  • Manual Backup: Create immediate backup
  • Scheduled Backups: Configure automatic backups (cron expression)
  • Backup Retention: Set number of backups to keep
  • Restore: Restore from any backup (requires restart)

Dangerous Operations:

  • Clear Column Family: Delete all data in a column family
  • Requires confirmation dialog
  • Cannot be undone

docs.title: Authentication & Credentials
docs.text: This section covers credential management for database connections and repository access.

docs.title: Kerberos Configuration
docs.text:
Scope: User-Scoped (All Users)

Manage Kerberos authentication credentials for secure database connections.

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text:
1. Keytab File - Upload keytab file - Principal automatically extracted from keytab - Keytab stored encrypted in RocksDB (not as file)

2. Password - Manual principal entry - Password stored encrypted

docs.title: Creating a Kerberos Configuration
docs.text:
1. Click "Add Configuration"
  2. Enter a configuration name
  3. Choose authentication method:
  4. Keytab: Upload keytab file, principal auto-detected
  5. Password: Enter principal and password manually
  6. Optionally specify custom krb5.conf path
  7. Save and optionally test authentication

docs.title: Usage with Connections
docs.text:
1. Create Kerberos configuration first
  2. In connection setup, select the Kerberos configuration
  3. Connection will use Subject.doAs() for user isolation

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use keytab authentication for automated/service connections
  • Set a default configuration for convenience
  • Keytabs are encrypted at rest for security
  • Each user's keytabs are isolated from other users

docs.title: Git Credentials
docs.text:
Scope: User-Scoped (All Users)

Store encrypted Git credentials for repository access.

docs.title: Credential Types
docs.text: Type Data Required Use Case SSH Key Private key content GitHub/GitLab SSH access Personal Access Token Token string HTTPS authentication Username & Password Username + Password Basic HTTPS auth

docs.title: Creating Credentials
docs.text:
1. Click "Add Credential"
  2. Enter a descriptive name
  3. Select credential type
  4. Provide credential data:
  5. SSH Key: Paste private key content
  6. PAT: Paste token
  7. Username/Password: Enter both fields
  8. Optionally specify host (e.g., github.com)
  9. Save

docs.title: Credential Features
docs.text:
• Encryption: All credentials encrypted at rest
  • Default Setting: Mark one credential as default
  • Host Filtering: Credentials can target specific hosts
  • Usage Tracking: Last-used timestamp recorded

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text: Query Pulse includes industry-standard database benchmarks that can be added to your workspace for performance testing and analysis.

docs.title: What Are Benchmarks?
docs.text:
Benchmarks are standardized sets of database schemas and queries developed by the Transaction Processing Performance Council (TPC). They provide:

  • Consistent Testing: Industry-accepted standards for comparing database performance
  • Real-World Scenarios: Queries that represent actual business workloads
  • Comprehensive Coverage: Wide range of query patterns and complexity levels

docs.title: TPC-DS (Decision Support Benchmark)
docs.text:
The TPC-DS benchmark is designed for measuring decision support system performance with complex, real-world analytical queries.

Attribute Value Tables 24 Queries 99 Focus Retail data warehouse analytics

Schema Overview:

TPC-DS models a retail product supplier with three sales channels:

  • Store Channel: Physical retail stores for in-person purchases
  • Catalog Channel: Mail-order catalog with phone/call center ordering
  • Web Channel: E-commerce website for online purchases

The schema includes:

  • 7 Fact Tables: inventory, store_sales, store_returns, catalog_sales, catalog_returns, web_sales, web_returns
  • 17 Dimension Tables: date_dim, time_dim, customer, customer_address, customer_demographics, household_demographics, item, promotion, store, call_center, catalog_page, web_site, web_page, warehouse, ship_mode, reason, income_band

Schema Files:

File Description create_all_tables_w_constraints.sql Standard schema DDL with primary/foreign key constraints create_all_tables_w_constraints_documented.sql Fully documented schema with comprehensive column-level comments add_comments.sql ALTER TABLE statements to add comments to existing tables (for use with pre-existing TPC-DS databases)

Choosing the Right Schema File:

  • New databases: Use create_all_tables_w_constraints_documented.sql to create tables with inline comments
  • Existing databases: Use add_comments.sql to add comments to tables that already exist (uses ALTER TABLE with the ${DB} variable)

The add_comments.sql script is useful when you already have TPC-DS data loaded in databases like tpcds_text_100 or tpcds_bin_partitioned_orc_100 and want to enhance them with documentation:

# Example: Add comments to existing TPC-DS database
hive --hivevar DB=tpcds_text_100 -f add_comments.sql

Documented Schema for AI-Assisted SQL:

The documented schema file includes rich metadata designed to enhance AI/LLM-assisted SQL generation:

  • Table-level comments: Business purpose, grain (what each row represents), and typical analysis use cases
  • Column-level comments: Semantic meaning, value formats, calculation formulas, and foreign key relationships
  • Business context: Explanations like "may differ for gifts" on shipping vs billing customer fields

Example of documentation enhancement:

-- Standard schema:
d_same_day_ly  INT,

-- Documented schema:
d_same_day_ly  INT  COMMENT 'Surrogate key of the same day last year, enables year-over-year comparisons',

Benefits for Agentic SQL Creation:

Benefit Description Semantic Understanding AI can understand business context without extensive prompting Accurate Joins Foreign key comments explain relationships, enabling correct join identification Query Explanations Rich metadata supports meaningful explanations to end users Reduced Errors Clear column semantics prevent misuse (e.g., using surrogate key vs natural key)

This demonstrates how well-documented schemas can significantly improve the accuracy and usefulness of AI-generated SQL queries in analytical workflows.

Query Categories:

Category Queries Description Reporting 19 Standard business reports and operational queries Ad-hoc 36 One-time analytical and exploratory queries Iterative 32 Drill-down, roll-up, and slice-and-dice patterns Data Mining 12 Complex analytical and pattern discovery queries

docs.title: Method 1: Workspace Settings Panel
docs.text:
1. Open your workspace
  2. Click the Settings icon in the workspace toolbar
  3. Select the Benchmarks tab
  4. Choose the benchmark you want to add (TPC-DS)
  5. Configure options:
  6. Database Name: Enter the target database name (optional)
  7. Include Schema: Check to add table creation scripts
  8. Include Queries: Check to add benchmark queries
  9. Click Setup Benchmark
  10. Refresh the file browser to see the new files

docs.title: Method 2: Benchmark Setup Modal
docs.text:
1. Open your workspace
  2. Click the Add Benchmark button in the workspace panel
  3. Select the benchmark type
  4. Configure the same options as above
  5. Click Add to Workspace

docs.title: Workspace Structure After Setup
docs.text:
When you add a benchmark, Query Pulse creates an organized directory structure:

workspace/
└── tpcds/
    ├── schema/
    │   ├── create_all_tables_w_constraints.sql
    │   ├── create_all_tables_w_constraints_documented.sql
    │   └── add_comments.sql
    └── queries/
        ├── reporting/
        │   ├── query01.sql
        │   ├── query02.sql
        │   └── ...
        ├── ad_hoc/
        │   ├── query03.sql
        │   └── ...
        ├── iterative/
        │   ├── query04.sql
        │   └── ...
        └── data_mining/
            ├── query05.sql
            └── ...

docs.title: Database Variable
docs.text:
When you specify a database name during setup, Query Pulse:

  1. Creates a workspace variable named DATABASE
  2. Sets its value to your specified database name
  3. All benchmark SQL files use ${DATABASE} placeholder

This allows you to: - Run queries against different databases by changing one variable - Avoid hardcoding database names in SQL files - Easily switch between development and production environments

Example usage in queries: 

SELECT * FROM ${DATABASE}.store_sales
WHERE ss_sold_date_sk = 2451545;

docs.title: Running Benchmark Queries
docs.text:
After adding a benchmark to your workspace:

  1. Create the Schema (if needed):
  2. Open tpcds/schema/create_all_tables_w_constraints.sql
  3. Ensure the DATABASE variable is set correctly

  4. Execute the script to create all tables

  5. Load Data:

  6. Use your preferred method to load TPC-DS data into the tables

  7. Data generators are available from the TPC website

  8. Run Queries:

  9. Open individual query files from the queries/ directories
  10. Execute queries to test performance
  11. Use the real-time progress tracking to monitor execution

docs.title: Setup Results
docs.text:
After benchmark setup, you'll see a confirmation showing:

  • Number of schema files created
  • Number of query files created
  • List of created file paths
  • Variable added (if database name was specified)

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start with Schema: Always create the schema before running queries
  2. Use Variables: Leverage the ${DATABASE} variable for flexibility
  3. Category Selection: Start with simpler reporting queries before complex data mining queries
  4. Monitor Progress: Use Query Pulse's real-time feedback to track long-running benchmark queries
  5. Compare Results: Run benchmarks on different configurations to compare performance

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Performance Baseline: Establish baseline performance metrics for your cluster
  • Configuration Tuning: Test different Hive/Trino configurations
  • Hardware Comparison: Compare performance across different hardware
  • Regression Testing: Verify performance after upgrades or changes
  • Learning: Explore complex SQL patterns used in real-world analytics

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Discovery
docs.text: Cloudera Manager (CM) Discovery automates the creation of HiveServer2 connections by querying Cloudera Manager's API for cluster configurations. Instead of manually constructing JDBC URLs with the correct ports, transport modes, SSL settings, and Kerberos principals, you can discover and import them directly.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Cloudera Manager API
       │
       │  /api/v41/clusters
       │  /api/v41/clusters/{name}/services
       │  /api/v41/clusters/{name}/services/{name}/config
       │  /api/v41/clusters/{name}/services/{name}/roles
       ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  Query Pulse Discovery      │
│                             │
│  1. Enumerate clusters      │
│  2. Find Hive services      │
│  3. Read HS2 configuration  │
│  4. Detect Kerberos/SSL     │
│  5. Build JDBC URLs         │
└──────────────┬──────────────┘
               │
               ▼
     User selects endpoints
     to import as connections

Discovery reads the following from each cluster's Hive service configuration:

Setting What It Determines hive_server2_transport_mode Binary (port 10000) or HTTP (port 10001) transport ssl_enabled Whether SSL/TLS is required hive_server2_authentication Kerberos or no authentication kerberos_princ_name Service principal for Kerberos connections dynamic_service_discovery ZooKeeper-based HA discovery vs direct host hive_zookeeper_quorum ZooKeeper connection string for HA hive_server2_zookeeper_namespace ZooKeeper namespace (default: hiveserver2) hs2_thrift_address_port Binary transport port hive_server2_thrift_http_port HTTP transport port hive_server2_thrift_http_path HTTP path (default: cliservice)

docs.title: CM Connections (Admin Setup)
docs.text:
Scope: Global (Admin Only)

Before users can discover endpoints, an administrator must configure one or more Cloudera Manager connections. These are global — all users share the same CM connections for discovery.

docs.title: Creating a CM Connection
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Settings > Admin > Discovery
  2. Click Add CM Connection
  3. Fill in the connection details:
Field Description Example Name Display name for this CM instance Production CM Description Optional notes CDP 7.1.9 Production Cluster CM URL Base URL of Cloudera Manager https://cm-host:7183 Username CM API username admin Password CM API password (encrypted at rest) Use TLS Enable HTTPS for API calls Enabled for port 7183 Skip TLS Verify Bypass certificate validation Use only for self-signed certs

Skip TLS Verify

Disabling TLS verification is a security risk. Use this only in development or when connecting to CM instances with self-signed certificates that cannot be added to the truststore.

docs.title: Testing Connectivity
docs.text:
Click Test on any CM connection to verify that Query Pulse can reach the Cloudera Manager API. The connection status displays:

  • SUCCESS — API is reachable and credentials are valid
  • FAILED — Check the URL, credentials, or network connectivity

docs.title: URL Normalization
docs.text:
The CM URL is automatically normalized. Trailing paths like /cmf or /cm are stripped, so all of these are equivalent:

  • https://cm-host:7183
  • https://cm-host:7183/cmf
  • https://cm-host:7183/cm

docs.title: Discovering Endpoints (User Workflow)
docs.text:
Scope: User-Scoped (All Users)

Any user can discover and import connections from the CM instances configured by an administrator.

docs.title: Step 1: Open the Import Modal
docs.text:
1. Open the Connections panel
  2. Click Import from CM
  3. Select a CM connection from the list and click Discover

docs.title: Step 2: Review Discovered Endpoints
docs.text:
Discovery queries each cluster and returns a list of HiveServer2 endpoints. Each entry shows:

  • Cluster name and version
  • Platform type (CDP 7.1, CDH 6, etc.)
  • Connection mode — ZooKeeper-based (HA) or direct host
  • Configuration badges:
    • Kerberos — Authentication requires a Kerberos principal
    • SSL — Connection uses SSL/TLS encryption
    • HTTP — Uses HTTP transport mode (vs binary)
    • ZooKeeper — Uses ZooKeeper service discovery for high availability

docs.title: Step 3: Select and Import
docs.text:
1. Select the endpoints you want to import (or use Select All)
  2. If any selected endpoint is kerberized, choose a Kerberos Configuration to associate with the imported connections
  3. Click Import

docs.title: Import Results
docs.text:
After import, you'll see a summary:

  • Created — New connections added to your account
  • Skipped — Connections that already exist (matched by name)
  • Warnings — Non-fatal issues (e.g., kerberized connection without a Kerberos config)
  • Errors — Connections that failed to import

Imported connections start with an UNTESTED status. Use Test in the Connections panel to verify connectivity.

docs.title: ZooKeeper-Based Discovery (HA)
docs.text:
When HiveServer2 is configured for ZooKeeper service discovery, the imported JDBC URL uses the ZK quorum:

jdbc:hive2://zk1:2181,zk2:2181,zk3:2181/;serviceDiscoveryMode=zooKeeper;zooKeeperNamespace=hiveserver2

This provides automatic failover — the client discovers available HS2 instances through ZooKeeper at connect time.

docs.title: Direct Host Discovery
docs.text:
When ZooKeeper discovery is not enabled, each HiveServer2 role is discovered as a separate endpoint with its hostname and port:

# Binary transport
jdbc:hive2://hs2-host.example.com:10000/default

# HTTP transport
jdbc:hive2://hs2-host.example.com:10001/default;transportMode=http;httpPath=cliservice

If a cluster has multiple HS2 roles, each one appears as a separate discoverable endpoint.

docs.title: Platform Detection
docs.text:
Discovery automatically maps the Cloudera cluster version to the correct Query Pulse platform type, which determines the appropriate JDBC driver:

Cluster Version Platform Type JDBC Driver CDH 5.x CDH 5 CDH 5 Hive CDH 6.x CDH 6 CDH 6 Hive CDP 7.0.x CDP 7.0 CDP 7.1.9 Hive CDP 7.1.x CDP 7.1 CDP 7.1.9 Hive CDP 7.1.9 SP1 CDP 7.1.9 SP1 CDP 7.1.9 Hive CDP 7.2.x CDP 7.2 CDP 7.3.1 Hive CDP 7.3.x CDP 7.3 CDP 7.3.1 Hive

Note

Ensure the corresponding JDBC driver is installed (via Settings > Admin > JDBC Drivers) before testing imported connections.

docs.title: Kerberos Handling
docs.text:
When discovery detects that hive_server2_authentication is set to KERBEROS, it:

  1. Extracts the service principal from the kerberos_princ_name configuration
  2. Reads the Kerberos realm from Cloudera Manager's SECURITY_REALM setting
  3. Constructs the full principal (e.g., hive/_HOST@EXAMPLE.COM)
  4. Includes the principal in the generated JDBC URL

At import time, you can associate a Kerberos Configuration (keytab + principal) with the imported connections. If no Kerberos configuration is available, the connection is still imported with a warning — you can configure Kerberos authentication later in the Connections panel.

docs.title: SSL/TLS Handling
docs.text:
When ssl_enabled is detected in the Hive service configuration, imported connections include SSL parameters in the JDBC URL. The exact parameters depend on the transport mode:

# Binary transport with SSL
jdbc:hive2://host:10000/default;ssl=true;principal=hive/_HOST@REALM

# HTTP transport with SSL
jdbc:hive2://host:10001/default;ssl=true;transportMode=http;httpPath=cliservice;principal=hive/_HOST@REALM

If your HS2 instances use certificates from a private CA, you may need to configure a TLS truststore in the connection settings after import.

docs.title: Discovery returns no endpoints
docs.text:
• Verify the CM connection test passes
  • Confirm the cluster has a Hive or Hive on Tez service
  • Check that the CM API user has read access to cluster configurations

docs.title: "HTML login page" error
docs.text:
The CM URL may be incorrect or the credentials are invalid. Cloudera Manager returns an HTML login page instead of a JSON API response when authentication fails. Verify:

  • The URL points to the CM API (typically port 7180 or 7183)
  • The username and password are correct
  • The user has API access permissions

docs.title: Imported connection fails to connect
docs.text:
• Ensure the correct JDBC driver is installed for the detected platform
  • For Kerberos connections, verify a valid Kerberos configuration is associated and the keytab/principal are correct
  • For SSL connections, check that the client trusts the HS2 server certificate
  • Test network connectivity to the HS2 host and port

docs.title: SQL Code Assist
docs.text: Query Pulse provides intelligent SQL autocompletion to help you write queries faster and with fewer errors. Code Assist understands your database schema and provides context-aware suggestions as you type.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Code Assist is enabled by default. Simply start typing in the SQL editor, and suggestions will appear automatically.

docs.title: Triggering Completions
docs.text:
Completions appear automatically when you:

  • Type after SQL keywords like SELECT, FROM, WHERE
  • Type a table alias followed by a dot (e.g., e.)
  • Start typing any identifier

You can also press Ctrl+Space (or Cmd+Space on Mac) to manually trigger completions.

docs.title: Accepting Completions
docs.text:
By default, press Tab to accept the highlighted suggestion. You can change this to Enter in your preferences.

To navigate suggestions:

  • Up/Down arrows: Move through the list
  • Tab or Enter: Accept the selected suggestion
  • Escape: Dismiss the suggestions

docs.title: Completion Types
docs.text:
Code Assist provides several types of suggestions:

Icon Type Description keyword Keywords SQL keywords like SELECT, FROM, WHERE, JOIN function Functions Built-in SQL functions like CONCAT, SUM, AVG class Tables Table names from your database property Columns Column names from tables namespace Databases Database/schema names

docs.title: Context-Aware Suggestions
docs.text: Code Assist understands the context of your query and provides relevant suggestions.

docs.title: After SELECT
docs.text:
When typing after SELECT, you'll see:

  • Column names from tables in your query
  • SQL functions
  • Keywords like DISTINCT, AS
SELECT | -- Shows columns from tables in FROM clause, functions, keywords
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id

docs.title: After FROM or JOIN
docs.text:
When typing after FROM or JOIN, you'll see table names from your database:

SELECT *
FROM | -- Shows available tables

docs.title: After Table Alias with Dot
docs.text:
When you type a table alias followed by a dot, you'll see only columns from that specific table:

SELECT e.| -- Shows only columns from 'employees' table
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id

docs.title: Smart Column Filtering
docs.text:
Code Assist automatically filters column suggestions based on the tables in your query:

Without FROM clause: 

SELECT | -- Shows all columns from all tables

With FROM clause: 

SELECT |
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id
-- Shows only columns from employees and departments

This makes it easier to find relevant columns, especially in databases with many tables.

docs.title: Dialect Support
docs.text: Code Assist supports both Hive and Trino SQL dialects. The dialect is automatically detected based on your connection type.

docs.title: Hive-Specific Features
docs.text:
• Hive keywords: LATERAL VIEW, EXPLODE, POSEXPLODE
  • Partitioning keywords: PARTITIONED BY, CLUSTERED BY
  • Hive functions: String, aggregate, and collection functions

docs.title: Trino-Specific Features
docs.text:
• Trino keywords: CATALOG, MERGE, PREPARE, EXECUTE
  • Extended SQL syntax: FETCH FIRST, ROWS ONLY
  • Trino functions: Extended SQL standard functions

docs.title: Schema Loading
docs.text: For table and column suggestions to work, Code Assist needs to load your database schema.

docs.title: Schema Status Indicator
docs.text:
The SQL editor shows the schema loading status:

Status Meaning Loading... Schema is being loaded from the database Ready Schema loaded successfully Warning No database selected (run USE database_name) Disabled Schema assist disabled by administrator

docs.title: Refreshing Schema
docs.text:
The schema cache is automatically refreshed periodically (default: every 24 hours). If you've made changes to your database schema, the updated schema will be available after the next refresh.

If you need immediate updates, contact your administrator to manually refresh the cache.

docs.title: User Preferences
docs.text: You can customize Code Assist behavior in your preferences.

docs.title: Accessing Preferences
docs.text:
1. Click your username in the top-right corner
  2. Select Preferences from the dropdown
  3. Scroll to the Code Assist section

docs.title: Available Settings
docs.text: Setting Options Description SQL Code Assist On/Off Enable or disable all SQL suggestions Schema Code Assist On/Off Enable or disable table/column suggestions Completion Accept Key Tab/Enter Key to accept suggestions

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text:
• Keep SQL Code Assist enabled for keywords and functions
  • Keep Schema Code Assist enabled if you work with unfamiliar databases
  • Use Tab for accept if you frequently use Enter to execute queries

docs.title: No Suggestions Appearing
docs.text:
1. Check your preferences: Ensure SQL Code Assist is enabled
  2. Check your connection: You need an active session for schema suggestions
  3. Select a database: Run USE database_name if you see a warning
  4. Wait for loading: Schema may still be loading for large databases

docs.title: Missing Tables or Columns
docs.text:
1. Schema may be cached: Wait for the automatic refresh or contact your administrator
  2. Check permissions: You may not have access to certain tables
  3. Check the database: Ensure you're connected to the correct database

docs.title: Slow Suggestions
docs.text:
1. Large schemas take longer: First load may be slow for databases with many tables
  2. Network latency: Suggestions require a round-trip to the server
  3. Subsequent requests are faster: Schema is cached after first load

docs.title: Wrong Dialect
docs.text:
If you see keywords for the wrong SQL dialect:

  1. Check your connection configuration
  2. Ensure the connection type (Hive/Trino) is correctly set
  3. Reconnect if you changed the connection settings

docs.title: Use Table Aliases
docs.text:
Using short aliases makes it faster to get column suggestions:

-- Good: Use short aliases
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id

-- Less efficient: Typing full table names
SELECT employees.name, departments.dept_name
FROM employees
JOIN departments ON employees.dept_id = departments.id

docs.title: Start with FROM
docs.text:
If you're exploring an unfamiliar database, start by typing the FROM clause:

FROM | -- See available tables

Then go back and add SELECT with context-aware column suggestions.

docs.title: Use Keywords for Navigation
docs.text:
Type SQL keywords to narrow suggestions:

SELECT * FROM employees WHERE | -- Shows columns for WHERE conditions
SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY | -- Shows columns for ordering
SELECT * FROM employees GROUP BY | -- Shows columns for grouping

docs.title: Administrator Notes
docs.text:
Administrators can configure Code Assist settings for all users:

  • Schema Cache TTL: How long schema metadata is cached (1 hour to 7 days)
  • Schema Assist Toggle: Enable or disable schema-aware completions globally

See the Administration Guide for details on managing these settings.

docs.title: Connectivity & Data Access
docs.text: This section covers database driver management and connection configuration.

docs.title: JDBC Drivers
docs.text:
Scope: Global (Admin Only)

The JDBC Drivers panel manages database driver downloads for supported connection types.

docs.title: Connection Type Tabs
docs.text: Drivers are organized by connection type: - Hive (Currently Available) - Multiple driver versions for different platforms - Impala (Coming Soon) - Placeholder for future support - Trino (Coming Soon) - Placeholder for future support

docs.title: Available Hive Drivers
docs.text: Driver Platforms Supported Apache Hive 2 Apache Hive 2 Apache Hive 3 EMR 6/7, Apache Hive 3 Apache Hive 4 Apache Hive 4 Apache Hive 4.1 Apache Hive 4 HDP 2 Hive HDP 2 CDH 5 Hive CDH 5 CDH 6 Hive CDH 6 CDP 7.1.9 Hive HDP 3, CDP 7.0, CDP 7.1, CDP 7.1.9 SP1 CDP 7.3.1 Hive CDP 7.2, CDP 7.3

docs.title: Driver Management
docs.text:
• Install: Download driver JARs from Maven repositories
  • Re-install: Refresh driver installation (delete and re-download)
  • Status: View installation status and installed files
  • Location: Drivers stored in ~/.query-pulse/drivers/

docs.title: Usage Notes
docs.text:
1. Install the appropriate driver for your target platform before creating connections
  2. Multiple drivers can be installed simultaneously
  3. Platform selection in connections determines which driver is used

docs.title: Connections
docs.text:
Scope: User-Scoped (All Users)

The Connections panel manages HiveServer2 database connections.

docs.title: Creating a Connection
docs.text:
1. Click "Add Connection"
  2. Provide connection details:
  3. Connection Name: Descriptive name
  4. Platform Type: Select your Hadoop/Hive distribution
  5. JDBC URL: Full HiveServer2 JDBC URL with parameters

docs.title: Supported Platforms
docs.text: Platform Hive Version Pool Type Apache Hive 2 Hive 2 DBCP2 (Legacy) CDH 5 Hive 2 DBCP2 (Legacy) CDH 6 Hive 2 DBCP2 (Legacy) HDP 2 Hive 2 DBCP2 (Legacy) HDP 3 Hive 3 Hikari CDP 7.0 - 7.3 Hive 3/4 Hikari EMR 6/7 Hive 3 Hikari Apache Hive 3/4 Hive 3/4 Hikari

docs.title: JDBC URL Examples
docs.text:
# Kerberos Authentication
jdbc:hive2://hiveserver2.example.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@REALM.COM

# Username/Password
jdbc:hive2://hiveserver2.example.com:10000/default

# Knox Gateway (SSL)
jdbc:hive2://knox-gateway.example.com:8443/;ssl=true;sslTrustStore=/path/to/truststore.jks;trustStorePassword=changeit;transportMode=http;httpPath=gateway/cdp-proxy-api/hive

# ZooKeeper Discovery (HA)
jdbc:hive2://zk1.example.com:2181,zk2.example.com:2181,zk3.example.com:2181/;serviceDiscoveryMode=zooKeeper;zooKeeperNamespace=hiveserver2

docs.title: Authentication Types
docs.text: Type Description URL Detection None No authentication Default Simple Username only Manual selection LDAP Username and password Manual selection Kerberos Principal-based Auto-detected from URL

docs.title: Kerberos Integration
docs.text: When principal is detected in the JDBC URL: - Authentication automatically switches to Kerberos mode - Option to select a stored Kerberos configuration - Or use external kinit for ticket management

docs.title: Connection Testing
docs.text:
• Save: Save connection without testing
  • Save & Test: Save then test connectivity using stored configuration
  • Test: Test existing connection

Connection status values: - VALID: Last test successful - INVALID: Last test failed (error message displayed) - UNTESTED: Never tested

docs.title: Driver Management
docs.text: Query Pulse provides comprehensive JDBC driver management, automatically downloading and configuring drivers for different database platforms and versions.

docs.title: Why Driver Management Matters
docs.text:
Connecting to Hive, Impala, or Trino requires the correct JDBC driver for your specific platform and version. Mismatched drivers cause: - Connection failures - Authentication errors - Missing features - Compatibility issues

Query Pulse eliminates driver complexity by: - Providing pre-configured driver definitions for all major platforms - Downloading drivers automatically from Maven repositories - Managing multiple driver versions simultaneously - Isolating drivers to prevent JAR conflicts

docs.title: Hive Drivers
docs.text: Driver Version Platforms Supported Apache Hive 2 2.3.10 Apache Hive 2 Apache Hive 3 3.1.3 Apache Hive 3, EMR 6/7 Apache Hive 4 4.0.1 Apache Hive 4 Apache Hive 4.1 4.1.0 Apache Hive 4.1 HDP 2 1.2.1000 Hortonworks HDP 2 CDH 5 1.1.0-cdh5 Cloudera CDH 5 CDH 6 2.1.1-cdh6 Cloudera CDH 6 CDP 7.1.9 3.1.3000 CDP 7.0, 7.1, 7.1.9, HDP 3 CDP 7.3.1 3.1.3000 CDP 7.2, 7.3

docs.title: Trino Drivers
docs.text: Trino drivers support version-aware management: - Default version: 446 - Any published version can be downloaded - Multiple versions can be installed simultaneously

docs.title: Driver Storage
docs.text:
Drivers are stored locally for offline access:

~/.query-pulse/drivers/
├── hive/
│   ├── 2/                    # Apache Hive 2
│   ├── 3/                    # Apache Hive 3
│   └── 4/                    # Apache Hive 4
├── cdh/
│   ├── 5/                    # CDH 5
│   └── 6/                    # CDH 6
├── hdp/
│   └── 2/                    # HDP 2
├── cdp/
│   ├── 7.1.9/               # CDP 7.1.9
│   └── 7.3.1/               # CDP 7.3.1
└── trino/
    ├── 446/                  # Trino 446
    └── 450/                  # Trino 450

docs.title: Access Driver Management
docs.text:
Admin Access Required: Only administrators can manage drivers.

  1. Click the Settings gear icon
  2. Select "JDBC Drivers" from Admin Settings

docs.title: Download a Driver
docs.text:
1. Select the dialect tab (Hive or Trino)
  2. Find the driver for your platform
  3. Click "Install"
  4. Wait for download to complete
  5. Green checkmark indicates successful installation

docs.title: Driver Dependencies
docs.text:
Each Hive driver downloads multiple JARs: - JDBC Driver: Main Hive JDBC standalone JAR - Hadoop Common: Core Hadoop classes - Hadoop Auth: Kerberos authentication support - Log4j 2: Logging dependencies

Trino drivers download a single JAR containing all dependencies.

docs.title: Refresh a Driver
docs.text:
To re-download a driver (e.g., after corruption):

  1. Find the installed driver
  2. Click "Refresh"
  3. Existing files are deleted and re-downloaded

docs.title: Maven Repository Integration
docs.text:
Drivers are downloaded from: - Maven Central: Standard open-source JARs - Cloudera Repository: CDP/CDH-specific JARs

Download progress is displayed during installation.

docs.title: Available Versions
docs.text: Query Pulse fetches the list of available Trino versions from Maven Central metadata.

docs.title: Installing a Specific Version
docs.text:
1. Select the Trino tab
  2. Choose a version from the dropdown
  3. Click "Install"

docs.title: Multiple Versions
docs.text: Install multiple Trino versions to support different clusters: - Each version is stored in a separate directory - Connections can specify which version to use - Class loaders are isolated per version

docs.title: Platform to Driver Mapping
docs.text:
When creating connections, select your platform and Query Pulse automatically uses the correct driver:

Platform Driver Used Apache Hive 2 Apache Hive 2 Apache Hive 3 Apache Hive 3 Apache Hive 4 Apache Hive 4 CDH 5 CDH 5 Hive CDH 6 CDH 6 Hive HDP 2 HDP 2 Hive HDP 3 CDP 7.1.9 Hive CDP 7.0-7.1.9 CDP 7.1.9 Hive CDP 7.2-7.3 CDP 7.3.1 Hive EMR 6/7 Apache Hive 3 Trino Trino (selected version)

docs.title: Class Loader Isolation
docs.text:
Query Pulse uses isolated class loaders for each driver:

Benefits: - Prevent JAR conflicts between drivers - Support multiple driver versions simultaneously - Isolate driver dependencies from application code

How It Works: 1. Each driver type gets its own URLClassLoader 2. Driver JARs are loaded in isolation 3. Connections use the appropriate class loader 4. Class loaders are cached for performance

docs.title: Driver Status
docs.text:
Check driver installation status:

Status Indicator Meaning Installed Green checkmark Driver ready to use Not Installed No indicator Driver needs download Installing Progress spinner Download in progress Error Red X Installation failed

docs.title: Driver Not Found
docs.text: If connections fail with "driver not found": 1. Open Driver Management 2. Verify the correct driver is installed 3. Click Refresh to re-download if needed

docs.title: Version Mismatch
docs.text: If you see compatibility errors: 1. Check your cluster's actual Hive/Trino version 2. Select the matching platform in connections 3. Ensure the correct driver is installed

docs.title: Download Failures
docs.text: If downloads fail: 1. Check internet connectivity 2. Verify firewall allows Maven repository access 3. Try refreshing the driver

docs.title: Class Loading Errors
docs.text: If class loading fails: 1. Refresh the driver to re-download JARs 2. Restart the application to clear class loader cache 3. Check for JAR corruption in the driver directory

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Install Before Connecting: Download drivers before creating connections
  2. Match Versions: Use drivers matching your cluster version
  3. Keep Updated: Refresh drivers when upgrading clusters
  4. Trino Versions: Install the version matching your Trino cluster
  5. Check Status: Verify green checkmarks before troubleshooting connections

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
The driver base directory can be configured:

app.driver.base-path=${user.home}/.query-pulse/drivers

Default Trino version:

trino.jdbc.version=446

docs.title: Hive Features
docs.text: Query Pulse provides comprehensive support for Apache Hive with real-time execution feedback, multiple explain plan visualizations, and detailed execution analytics.

docs.title: Real-Time Query Progress
docs.text: When executing queries against Hive connections, Query Pulse provides Beeline-style real-time progress tracking with detailed stage information.

docs.title: Progress Display
docs.text:
The progress display shows live updates during query execution:

  • Overall Progress: Percentage completion with visual progress bar
  • Current Stage: Description of the currently executing stage
  • Elapsed Time: Human-readable duration since execution started

docs.title: Stage-Based Tracking
docs.text:
Hive queries using Tez execution engine display detailed stage information:

Stage Type Description Map Map-side processing stages Reducer Reduce-side processing stages Vertex Tez vertex execution stages

Each stage displays task counters:

  • Total Tasks: Number of tasks in the stage
  • Completed: Successfully finished tasks
  • Running: Currently executing tasks
  • Pending: Tasks waiting to execute
  • Failed: Tasks that encountered errors
  • Killed: Tasks terminated before completion

docs.title: Stage Status Indicators
docs.text:
Stages are color-coded by status:

Status Color Description PENDING Gray Stage not yet started INITIALIZING Blue Stage initializing RUNNING Blue Stage actively executing COMPLETED Green Stage finished successfully FAILED Red Stage encountered an error KILLED Orange Stage was terminated

docs.title: View Modes
docs.text:
Toggle between two display modes in User Preferences:

  • Summary View: Single-line progress bar with percentage
  • Detailed View: Full stage breakdown with all task counters

docs.title: Explain Plan Features
docs.text: Query Pulse provides five different explain visualizations for Hive queries, each offering unique insights into query execution.

docs.title: Visual Explain (EXPLAIN FORMATTED)
docs.text:
Generates a graphical representation of the query execution plan.

How to Use: 1. Write or select a query in the SQL editor 2. Click the Visual Explain button (tree icon) 3. A DOT graph file is created and opened in a new editor tab

Output: Graphical execution plan showing: - Operators and their relationships - Data flow between stages - Join strategies - Filter pushdowns

Command Executed: EXPLAIN FORMATTED <query>

docs.title: Visual Cost (EXPLAIN CBO)
docs.text:
Shows the Cost-Based Optimizer's analysis of the query plan.

How to Use: 1. Write or select a query in the SQL editor 2. Click the Visual Cost button (chart icon) 3. A DOT graph file is created showing the optimized plan

Output: Calcite-based optimization plan displaying: - Cost estimates for each operation - Join ordering decisions - Predicate pushdown optimizations - Statistics-based decisions

Command Executed: EXPLAIN CBO <query>

docs.title: Explain Dependency
docs.text:
Analyzes table and partition dependencies for a query.

How to Use: 1. Write or select a query in the SQL editor 2. Click the Explain Dependency button (JSON icon) 3. Results display as formatted JSON in the results panel

Output: JSON structure showing: - Input tables required by the query - Partition dependencies - Database and table names - Dependency types

Command Executed: EXPLAIN DEPENDENCY <query>

Use Cases: - Understanding data lineage - Identifying partition pruning - Validating table access requirements

docs.title: Explain Authorization
docs.text:
Shows the permissions required to execute a query.

How to Use: 1. Write or select a query in the SQL editor 2. Click the Explain Authorization button (shield icon) 3. Results display as text in the results panel

Output: Authorization details including: - Required read privileges - Required write privileges - Owner information - Table-level permissions

Command Executed: EXPLAIN AUTHORIZATION <query>

Use Cases: - Security auditing - Permission troubleshooting - Access control validation

docs.title: Explain Vectorization
docs.text:
Analyzes vectorized execution capabilities for the query.

How to Use: 1. Write or select a query in the SQL editor 2. Click the Explain Vectorization button (parallel lines icon) 3. A popup appears with configuration options 4. Select options and click "Execute"

Configuration Options:

Option Description ONLY Mode Show only vectorization details (exclude other plan info) Detail Level SUMMARY, DETAIL, or VERBOSE

Detail Levels:

  • SUMMARY: High-level vectorization status
  • DETAIL: Operator-level vectorization information
  • VERBOSE: Complete vectorization details including expressions

Output: DOT graph showing: - Which operators are vectorized - Why certain operators cannot be vectorized - Expression vectorization status - Data type compatibility

Command Executed: EXPLAIN FORMATTED VECTORIZATION [ONLY] [SUMMARY|DETAIL|VERBOSE] <query>

docs.title: Execution Details
docs.text: After query completion, detailed execution information is available in the Execution Details panel.

docs.title: Query Metadata
docs.text: Field Description Query ID Hive-assigned query identifier DAG ID Tez DAG identifier Application ID YARN application ID Execution Time Total query duration Status Final query status

docs.title: Execution Summary
docs.text:
When hive.tez.exec.print.summary=true is configured, the execution summary displays:

Query Execution Summary: - Total MapReduce CPU time - Total HDFS bytes read/written - Total records processed

Task Execution Summary: - Per-vertex task counts - Duration statistics (min, max, mean) - Input/output record counts

Counter Sections: - HIVE counters - FileSystem counters - Shuffle error counters - Tez counters

docs.title: Supported Query Types
docs.text:
Explain features are available for the following statement types:

  • SELECT
  • INSERT
  • UPDATE
  • DELETE
  • MERGE

DDL statements (CREATE, ALTER, DROP) are not supported for explain operations.

docs.title: Variable Substitution
docs.text:
All explain operations support workspace and file variables:

-- Using variables in queries
SELECT * FROM ${database}.${table} WHERE date = '${run_date}'

Variables are substituted before the explain command is executed. If undefined variables are detected, a dialog prompts for values.

docs.title: File Output
docs.text:
Graphical explain operations create files in the workspace:

Operation File Pattern Extension Visual Explain {filename}_explain_{n} .dot Visual Cost {filename}_cbo_{n} .dot Vectorization {filename}_vectorization_{n} .dot

Files are automatically opened in new editor tabs for viewing.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use Visual Explain to understand the basic execution structure
  2. Use Visual Cost when investigating performance issues related to join ordering or statistics
  3. Use Explain Dependency before making table changes to understand impact
  4. Use Explain Authorization when troubleshooting permission errors
  5. Use Explain Vectorization when queries are slower than expected to check if vectorization is being used

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Installation
docs.text: Deploy Query Pulse as a managed service on a CDP cluster using a CSD (Custom Service Descriptor) and Parcel.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Cloudera Manager 7.0 or later
  • CDP cluster with at least one host available for the Query Pulse role
  • Admin access to Cloudera Manager

docs.title: Step 1 — Install the CSD
docs.text:
The CSD (Custom Service Descriptor) tells Cloudera Manager how to configure and manage the Query Pulse service. It must be installed before the parcel.

Obtain the CSD JAR (QUERY_PULSE-<version>.jar) from the release artifacts.

  1. Copy the CSD JAR to /opt/cloudera/csd/ on the Cloudera Manager host
  2. Restart the Cloudera Manager Server: 
    sudo systemctl restart cloudera-scm-server
  3. After restart, Query Pulse will appear as an available service type

docs.title: Step 2 — Install and Activate the Parcel
docs.text:
The parcel contains the Query Pulse application binaries that get distributed to cluster hosts.

Obtain the parcel (QUERY_PULSE-<version>-<distro>.parcel) and its SHA checksum file from the release artifacts.

  1. Copy the .parcel and .parcel.sha files to /opt/cloudera/parcel-repo/ on the Cloudera Manager host
  2. In Cloudera Manager, go to Parcels and click Check for New Parcels
  3. Find QUERY_PULSE in the list
  4. Click Distribute and wait for distribution to all hosts to complete
  5. Click Activate to make the parcel available for use

docs.title: Step 3 — Add the Service
docs.text:
1. In Cloudera Manager, go to your cluster and click Add Service
  2. Select Query Pulse from the service list
  3. Assign the Query Pulse Server role to a host
  4. Configure the service (see Configuration below)
  5. Click Start to launch the service

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: All settings are available in the Cloudera Manager UI under the Query Pulse service configuration.

docs.title: Core Settings
docs.text: Parameter Default Description Server Port 8088 HTTP/HTTPS port Context Path / URL context path Working Directory /var/lib/query-pulse Data and configuration directory Log Directory /var/log/query-pulse Application log files

docs.title: JVM Settings
docs.text: Parameter Default Description Heap Initial Size 2048 MB Initial JVM heap Heap Max Size 4096 MB Maximum JVM heap Garbage Collector G1GC GC algorithm (G1GC, ZGC, ParallelGC) Log Level INFO Application log verbosity

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Parameter Default Description Authentication Type local local, ldap, or oidc Admin Username admin Admin account username Reset Admin Password false Reset admin password on next start

LDAP and OIDC parameters are also configurable through the CM UI. See the Quick Start Guide for parameter details.

docs.title: Auto-TLS
docs.text:
When Auto-TLS is enabled on the cluster, Query Pulse automatically discovers and uses the cluster's keystores and truststores. The control script searches for:

  • cm-auto-host_keystore.jks — Server keystore
  • cm-auto-host_key.pw — Keystore password
  • cm-auto-in_cluster_truststore.jks — In-cluster truststore (preferred)
  • cm-auto-global_truststore.jks — Global truststore (fallback)

No manual truststore configuration is needed when Auto-TLS is active.

For custom certificates, configure the Client Truststore Location and Client Truststore Password in the SSL/TLS section of the service configuration.

docs.title: Web Links
docs.text:
After the service starts, Cloudera Manager provides quick links:

Link URL Query Pulse Web UI http://<host>:<port>/ API Docs (Swagger) http://<host>:<port>/swagger-ui.html Health Status http://<host>:<port>/actuator/health

docs.title: Service Management
docs.text:
Once deployed, manage Query Pulse through Cloudera Manager like any other service:

  • Start / Stop / Restart from the service Actions menu
  • View logs from the Query Pulse Server role Logs tab
  • Monitor health via the built-in health check
  • Update configuration and rolling restart when needed

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text:
To upgrade to a new version:

  1. Obtain the new parcel and CSD JAR from the release artifacts
  2. Replace the CSD JAR in /opt/cloudera/csd/ and restart CM Server
  3. Upload the new parcel to /opt/cloudera/parcel-repo/
  4. In Cloudera Manager: Distribute, Activate the new parcel, Deactivate the old one
  5. Restart the Query Pulse service

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution Service fails to start Check stderr log in CM role instance Logs tab Parcel not visible in CM Verify .parcel and .sha files are in /opt/cloudera/parcel-repo/ and click Check for New Parcels CSD not visible after install Restart the Cloudera Manager Server process SSL handshake failures Verify Auto-TLS is active or configure a custom truststore Java version error Ensure the host has JDK 17+ and the parcel's defines.sh points to it

docs.title: Docker Installation
docs.text: Run Query Pulse as a Docker container for quick evaluation or production deployment.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: • Docker 20.10 or later

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
docker run -d \
  --name query-pulse \
  -p 8088:8088 \
  -v ~/.query-pulse:/data/query-pulse \
  cloudera/query-pulse:latest

Open http://localhost:8088 in your browser.

docs.title: Volumes
docs.text:
Map a host directory to persist data across container restarts:

Host Path Container Path Purpose ~/.query-pulse /data/query-pulse Persistent data (database, drivers, security, settings)

To also map logs to a separate host directory:

docker run -d \
  --name query-pulse \
  -p 8088:8088 \
  -v ~/.query-pulse:/data/query-pulse \
  -v ~/.query-pulse/logs:/var/log/query-pulse \
  cloudera/query-pulse:latest

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Pass any QUERY_PULSE_* environment variable with -e:

docker run -d \
  --name query-pulse \
  -p 9000:9000 \
  -v ~/.query-pulse:/data/query-pulse \
  -e QUERY_PULSE_SERVER_PORT=9000 \
  -e QUERY_PULSE_HEAP_MAX=8192 \
  -e QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=ldap \
  cloudera/query-pulse:latest

The most common variables:

Variable Default Description QUERY_PULSE_SERVER_PORT 8088 HTTP/HTTPS port QUERY_PULSE_CONTEXT_PATH / URL context path QUERY_PULSE_HEAP_INIT 2048 Initial JVM heap (MB) QUERY_PULSE_HEAP_MAX 4096 Maximum JVM heap (MB) QUERY_PULSE_LOG_LEVEL INFO Log verbosity QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE local Authentication mode (local, ldap, oidc)

For the full list of authentication, LDAP, OIDC, and security variables, see the Quick Start Guide.

docs.title: Truststore for Self-Signed Certificates
docs.text:
Mount your truststore into the container and set the environment variables:

docker run -d \
  --name query-pulse \
  -p 8088:8088 \
  -v ~/.query-pulse:/data/query-pulse \
  -v /path/to/truststore.jks:/etc/pki/truststore.jks:ro \
  -e QUERY_PULSE_TRUSTSTORE_LOCATION=/etc/pki/truststore.jks \
  -e QUERY_PULSE_TRUSTSTORE_PASSWORD=changeit \
  cloudera/query-pulse:latest

See the Quick Start Guide for details on creating a truststore and uploading certificates through the UI.

docs.title: Kerberos
docs.text:
To use Kerberos authentication for database connections, mount the host Kerberos configuration:

docker run -d \
  --name query-pulse \
  -p 8088:8088 \
  -v ~/.query-pulse:/data/query-pulse \
  -v /etc/krb5.conf:/etc/krb5.conf:ro \
  cloudera/query-pulse:latest

docs.title: Managing the Container
docs.text:
docker start query-pulse-server      # Start
docker stop query-pulse-server       # Stop
docker restart query-pulse-server    # Restart
docker logs -f query-pulse-server    # Follow logs
docker rm query-pulse-server         # Remove

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
The container includes a built-in health check:

docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' query-pulse-server

Or query the health endpoint directly:

curl -f http://localhost:8088/actuator/health

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution Container exits immediately Check logs: docker logs query-pulse-server Port conflict Change QUERY_PULSE_SERVER_PORT and the -p port mapping Data lost after restart Ensure the /data/query-pulse volume is mapped to a persistent host path SSL connection failures Mount a truststore with the required CA certificates Out of memory Increase QUERY_PULSE_HEAP_MAX and the Docker --memory limit

docs.title: Standalone Installation
docs.text: Install Query Pulse directly on a Linux server or edge node.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Java JDK 17 or later
  • 4 GB RAM minimum, 8 GB recommended

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Download the Query Pulse release archive (query-pulse-install-<version>-dist.tar.gz).

Step 1 — Extract the archive:

tar zxvf query-pulse-install-1.3.0-dist.tar.gz
cd query-pulse-install-1.3.0

The archive contains:

query-pulse-install-1.3.0/
  setup.sh           # Installation script
  web.properties     # Default configuration
  bin/
    query-pulse.sh   # Startup script
  lib/
    query-pulse-1.3.0.jar

Step 2 — Run setup:

./setup.sh

The setup script validates your Java version (17+ required), then installs the application. The install location depends on whether you run as root or a regular user:

Run As Application Binaries Data & Configuration Regular user $HOME/.query-pulse/bin, lib $HOME/.query-pulse/conf, data, logs Root /usr/local/query-pulse/bin, lib $HOME/.query-pulse/conf, data, logs

When run as root, a symlink is created at /usr/local/bin/query-pulse. When run as a regular user, the symlink goes to $HOME/bin/query-pulse — add $HOME/bin to your PATH if it isn't already.

The setup preserves any existing web.properties in $HOME/.query-pulse/conf/, so re-running setup for an upgrade will not overwrite your configuration.

Step 3 — Start the application:

query-pulse start

Verify: Open http://<hostname>:8088 in your browser.

docs.title: Managing the Service
docs.text:
query-pulse start     # Start in the background
query-pulse stop      # Stop the service
query-pulse status    # Check if running

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Query Pulse uses two properties files, both managed through the admin UI under Settings > Administration. Changes to these files require a restart to take effect.

File Location Purpose web.properties $HOME/.query-pulse/settings/web.properties JVM, logging, authentication, and application settings tls.properties $HOME/.query-pulse/settings/tls.properties TLS/mTLS and certificate settings

Settings can also be overridden with environment variables:

export QUERY_PULSE_SERVER_PORT=8088        # HTTP port (default: 8088)
export QUERY_PULSE_HEAP_INIT=2048          # Initial heap in MB
export QUERY_PULSE_HEAP_MAX=4096           # Max heap in MB
export QUERY_PULSE_LOG_LEVEL=INFO          # DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
After installation and first run, the working directory contains:

$HOME/.query-pulse/
  bin/                # query-pulse.sh
  lib/                # Application JAR
  conf/               # web.properties (initial copy)
  settings/           # web.properties, tls.properties (managed by the app)
  data/               # RocksDB embedded database
  drivers/            # JDBC driver JARs
  security/           # Keystores and certificates
  logs/               # Application logs
  run/                # PID file

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text:
To upgrade to a new version:

  1. Stop the running instance: query-pulse stop
  2. Extract the new release archive
  3. Run ./setup.sh again — existing configuration is preserved
  4. Start the application: query-pulse start

docs.title: Truststore for Self-Signed Certificates
docs.text:
If your data sources or authentication endpoints use self-signed certificates, configure a system truststore:

export QUERY_PULSE_TRUSTSTORE_LOCATION=/path/to/truststore.jks
export QUERY_PULSE_TRUSTSTORE_PASSWORD=changeit

This sets the JVM system properties so all outbound SSL connections (JDBC, LDAP, OIDC) trust the certificates in this truststore.

You can also upload individual certificates through the UI under Settings > Certificates. See the Quick Start Guide for details on creating a truststore.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution query-pulse: command not found Add $HOME/bin to your PATH or use the full path: $HOME/.query-pulse/bin/query-pulse.sh Java version error during setup Install JDK 17+ and set JAVA_HOME Port 8088 already in use Set QUERY_PULSE_SERVER_PORT to a different port Application starts but UI is blank Wait 30-60 seconds for full startup; check logs in $HOME/.query-pulse/logs/ Cannot connect to data source Ensure the JDBC driver is installed and the truststore contains required certificates

docs.title: Key Design Principles
docs.text:
• Security First: All sensitive data (passwords, keytabs, API keys) is encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM
  • User Isolation: User-specific configurations are stored separately, ensuring data privacy
  • Role-Based Access: Admin users have access to global system settings; regular users manage their own configurations
  • Embedded Database: RocksDB provides fast, reliable local storage without external database dependencies

docs.title: LDAP Authentication
docs.text:
Scope: Global (Admin Only)

Configure Query Pulse to authenticate users against an LDAP or Active Directory server instead of the local user database.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
When LDAP authentication is enabled, non-admin users authenticate by verifying their credentials against your directory server. Query Pulse uses a two-phase bind: a service account searches for the user, then the user's own credentials are verified. The admin account always uses local authentication, ensuring access even if the LDAP server is unavailable.

Supported Directory Servers:

Server Search Filter Username Attribute Active Directory (sAMAccountName={0}) sAMAccountName FreeIPA (uid={0}) uid OpenLDAP (uid={0}) uid

docs.title: Enabling LDAP
docs.text:
Set the authentication type to ldap and provide your directory server details:

export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=ldap
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldap://ldap.example.com:389
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_DN="cn=serviceaccount,dc=example,dc=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_PASSWORD="service-password"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_BASE="ou=users,dc=example,dc=com"

Restart the application after changing authentication settings.

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: Setting Environment Variable Default Description LDAP URL QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL (required) Server URL. Use ldap:// for plain or ldaps:// for SSL Bind DN QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_DN (required) Service account distinguished name Bind Password QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_PASSWORD (required) Service account password User Search Base QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_BASE (required) Base DN where user searches begin User Search Filter QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_FILTER (uid={0}) Filter to find users; {0} is replaced with the username Username Attribute QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USERNAME_ATTR uid LDAP attribute containing the username Connect Timeout QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_CONNECT_TIMEOUT 5000 Connection timeout in milliseconds Read Timeout QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_READ_TIMEOUT 10000 Read timeout in milliseconds Use SSL QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_SSL false Use LDAPS (SSL on port 636) Use StartTLS QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_STARTTLS false Upgrade LDAP to TLS on port 389 Skip SSL Verify QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_SKIP_SSL_VERIFY false Skip certificate verification (testing only)

docs.title: Plain LDAP
docs.text:
QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldap://ldap.example.com:389
Credentials are transmitted in clear text. Not recommended for production.

docs.title: LDAPS (SSL)
docs.text:
QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldaps://ldap.example.com:636
QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_SSL=true
Full SSL/TLS encryption from connection start.

docs.title: StartTLS
docs.text:
QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldap://ldap.example.com:389
QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_STARTTLS=true
Starts with a plain connection, then upgrades to TLS. Useful when your server supports TLS on the standard LDAP port.

docs.title: Certificate Verification
docs.text:
By default, SSL connections verify the server certificate against the JVM truststore. If your LDAP server uses a self-signed or internal CA certificate, either:

  1. Import the CA certificate into the Query Pulse truststore (recommended)
  2. Set QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_SKIP_SSL_VERIFY=true (testing only)

docs.title: Active Directory
docs.text:
export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=ldap
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldap://ad.corp.example.com:389
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_DN="CN=svc-querypulse,CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=example,DC=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_PASSWORD="service-password"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_BASE="CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=example,DC=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_FILTER="(sAMAccountName={0})"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USERNAME_ATTR=sAMAccountName

docs.title: FreeIPA
docs.text:
export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=ldap
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldaps://ipa.example.com:636
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_SSL=true
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_DN="uid=svc-querypulse,cn=users,cn=accounts,dc=example,dc=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_PASSWORD="service-password"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_BASE="cn=users,cn=accounts,dc=example,dc=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_FILTER="(uid={0})"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USERNAME_ATTR=uid

docs.title: OpenLDAP
docs.text:
export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=ldap
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldap://ldap.example.com:389
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_DN="cn=readonly,dc=example,dc=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_PASSWORD="service-password"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_BASE="ou=people,dc=example,dc=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_FILTER="(uid={0})"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USERNAME_ATTR=uid

docs.title: Pre-Registration Required
docs.text:
Users must be registered in Query Pulse before they can log in with LDAP credentials. The workflow is:

  1. An admin or the user registers an account in Query Pulse (username must match the LDAP username attribute)
  2. The user logs in using their LDAP directory password
  3. The local password (set during registration) is not used for login when LDAP is enabled

docs.title: Admin Account
docs.text:
The admin account (default username: admin) always authenticates using its local password, regardless of LDAP configuration. This ensures:

  • Admin access is maintained when the LDAP server is unavailable
  • Emergency password reset via QUERY_PULSE_ADMIN_PASSWORD_RESET=true always works

docs.title: Account Lockout
docs.text: Failed login attempts are tracked even with LDAP authentication. After the configured number of failures (default: 5), the account is locked. An admin must re-enable the account.

docs.title: Docker Deployment
docs.text:
# docker-compose.yml
environment:
  - QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=ldap
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldap://ldap.example.com:389
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_DN=cn=admin,dc=example,dc=com
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_PASSWORD=password
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_BASE=ou=users,dc=example,dc=com
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_FILTER=(uid={0})
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USERNAME_ATTR=uid
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_SSL=false
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_STARTTLS=false
  - QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_SKIP_SSL_VERIFY=false

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Deployment
docs.text:
In the Query Pulse service configuration:

  1. Set Authentication Type to ldap
  2. Fill in the LDAP URL, Bind DN, Bind Password, and User Search Base
  3. Adjust the User Search Filter and Username Attribute for your directory type
  4. Enable SSL or StartTLS if required
  5. Save and restart the service

Note: In CSD configurations, use USERNAME_PLACEHOLDER instead of {0} in the search filter. Cloudera Manager will translate this automatically.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Cause Solution "Invalid credentials" for LDAP users User not registered locally Register the user in Query Pulse first Connection timeout LDAP URL or port incorrect Verify URL and network connectivity SSL handshake failure Certificate not trusted Import CA cert into truststore or enable skip verification for testing "User not found in directory" Wrong search base or filter Verify search base DN and filter match your directory structure Admin can't log in via LDAP Expected behavior Admin always uses local authentication
