docs.title: OIDC / SSO Authentication
docs.text:
Scope: Global (Admin Only)

Configure Query Pulse to authenticate users via an OpenID Connect (OIDC) Identity Provider for Single Sign-On (SSO).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
When OIDC authentication is enabled, the login page displays a "Login with SSO" button alongside the standard username/password form. Clicking the SSO button redirects users to your Identity Provider (IdP) for authentication. After successful login, the user is redirected back to Query Pulse with a valid session.

Supported Identity Providers:

Provider Issuer URI Pattern Keycloak https://keycloak.example.com/realms/{realm} Okta https://{domain}.okta.com/oauth2/default Azure AD https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant-id}/v2.0 Google https://accounts.google.com Any OIDC-compliant provider Must support .well-known/openid-configuration

Key Features:

  • Automatic endpoint discovery from issuer URI
  • Dynamic user provisioning on first login
  • Group-based access control
  • Group-based admin role assignment
  • Admin account can still use local password login

docs.title: Enabling OIDC
docs.text:
Set the authentication type to oidc and provide your Identity Provider details:

export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=oidc
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ISSUER_URI=https://keycloak.example.com/realms/myrealm
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=query-pulse
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret

Restart the application after changing authentication settings.

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: Setting Environment Variable Default Description Issuer URI QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ISSUER_URI (required) OIDC provider base URL Client ID QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID (required) OAuth2 client ID Client Secret QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET (required) OAuth2 client secret Scopes QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_SCOPES openid profile email groups Space-separated OAuth2 scopes User Group QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_USER_GROUP (empty) Required group for access; empty allows all Admin Group QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ADMIN_GROUP (empty) Group that grants ADMIN role; empty disables Dynamic User Create QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_DYNAMIC_USER_CREATE true Auto-create user on first OIDC login

docs.title: Authentication Flow
docs.text:
1. User visits the login page and clicks Login with SSO
  2. Browser redirects to the Identity Provider's login page
  3. User authenticates with the IdP (password, MFA, etc.)
  4. IdP redirects back to Query Pulse with an authorization code
  5. Query Pulse exchanges the code for OIDC tokens
  6. User information is extracted from the ID token:
  7. Username from preferred_username claim (falls back to email prefix, then subject)
  8. Email from email claim
  9. Groups from groups claim (falls back to roles claim)
  10. Group access is validated (if configured)
  11. A local user account is created or updated
  12. Internal JWT tokens are issued and the user is logged in

docs.title: Restricting Access to a Group
docs.text:
Set QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_USER_GROUP to require users to be a member of a specific group:

export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_USER_GROUP=query-pulse-users

Users not in this group will see an error: "You do not have permission to access this application."

Leave this empty to allow all authenticated users.

docs.title: Granting Admin Role via Group
docs.text:
Set QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ADMIN_GROUP to automatically grant the ADMIN role to members of a specific group:

export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ADMIN_GROUP=query-pulse-admins

Admin status is synced on each login. If a user is removed from the admin group in the IdP, their ADMIN role is revoked at next login. Leave this empty to manage admin roles manually.

docs.title: Dynamic User Creation (Default)
docs.text:
When QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_DYNAMIC_USER_CREATE=true (default), a local user account is automatically created the first time a user logs in via SSO. The account is created with:

  • Username from the OIDC preferred_username claim
  • Email from the OIDC email claim
  • USER role (and ADMIN role if in admin group)
  • A random password hash (OIDC users cannot log in with a password)

docs.title: Pre-Created Users
docs.text: When QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_DYNAMIC_USER_CREATE=false, users must be pre-created by an admin before they can log in via SSO. The local username must match the OIDC preferred_username claim.

docs.title: Keycloak
docs.text:
1. Create a new client in your realm:
  2. Client ID: query-pulse
  3. Client Protocol: openid-connect
  4. Access Type: confidential
  5. Set Valid Redirect URIs: https://query-pulse.example.com/login/oauth2/code/oidc
  6. Under Client Scopes, ensure groups scope is available
  7. Create a Group Membership mapper:
  8. Mapper Type: Group Membership
  9. Token Claim Name: groups
  10. Add to ID token: Yes
  11. Copy the client secret from the Credentials tab
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ISSUER_URI=https://keycloak.example.com/realms/myrealm
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=query-pulse
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET=<from-credentials-tab>

docs.title: Okta
docs.text:
1. Create a new Web Application in Okta
  2. Set Sign-in redirect URI: https://query-pulse.example.com/login/oauth2/code/oidc
  3. Set Sign-out redirect URI: https://query-pulse.example.com/login
  4. Under Sign On > OpenID Connect ID Token, add a groups claim:
  5. Claim name: groups
  6. Include in token type: ID Token
  7. Filter: Matches regex .*
  8. Note the Client ID and Client Secret
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ISSUER_URI=https://dev-12345.okta.com/oauth2/default
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=<client-id>
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET=<client-secret>

docs.title: Azure AD (Microsoft Entra ID)
docs.text:
1. Register a new application in Azure AD
  2. Under Authentication, add a Web platform redirect URI: https://query-pulse.example.com/login/oauth2/code/oidc
  3. Under Certificates & Secrets, create a new client secret
  4. Under Token Configuration, add a groups claim:
  5. Claim type: Groups
  6. Token type: ID
  7. Note the Application (client) ID and Directory (tenant) ID
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ISSUER_URI=https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/v2.0
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=<application-id>
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET=<client-secret>

docs.title: Admin Account with OIDC
docs.text:
The admin account (default username: admin) can always log in with local credentials, even when OIDC is the configured authentication type. Both the SSO button and the username/password form appear on the login page.

This provides:

  • Emergency admin access when the IdP is unavailable
  • Password reset capability via QUERY_PULSE_ADMIN_PASSWORD_RESET=true

docs.title: Docker Deployment
docs.text:
# docker-compose.yml
environment:
  - QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=oidc
  - QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ISSUER_URI=https://keycloak.example.com/realms/myrealm
  - QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=query-pulse
  - QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret
  - QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_SCOPES=openid profile email groups
  - QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_USER_GROUP=query-pulse-users
  - QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ADMIN_GROUP=query-pulse-admins
  - QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_DYNAMIC_USER_CREATE=true

docs.title: Cloudera Manager Deployment
docs.text:
In the Query Pulse service configuration:

  1. Set Authentication Type to oidc
  2. Fill in the OIDC Issuer URI, Client ID, and Client Secret
  3. Optionally configure User Group and Admin Group for access control
  4. Set Dynamic User Creation as desired
  5. Save and restart the service

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Cause Solution SSO button not visible OIDC not configured or config endpoint unreachable Verify issuer URI, client ID, and client secret are set "Access was denied by the identity provider" User denied access at IdP Check IdP user permissions and application assignment "You do not have permission" User not in required group Add user to the group specified by OIDC_USER_GROUP Redirect loop after login Callback URL mismatch Ensure IdP redirect URI matches https://<host>/login/oauth2/code/oidc User created without admin role User not in admin group Add user to the group specified by OIDC_ADMIN_GROUP "Authentication failed. No tokens received." Callback URL parameters missing Check IdP configuration and network connectivity Admin can't use SSO Expected behavior Admin uses local credentials; other users use SSO Provider discovery fails Issuer URI incorrect or unreachable Verify the URI returns a valid .well-known/openid-configuration

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get Query Pulse up and running in minutes. Choose your installation method, configure authentication, and connect to your first data source.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Requirement Details Java JDK 17 or later Memory 4 GB minimum, 8 GB recommended Disk 500 MB for the application, plus space for data and drivers Browser Chrome, Firefox, Edge, or Safari (modern versions) Network Access to your Hive, Trino, or Impala endpoints

docs.title: Installation Options
docs.text: Method Best For Guide Standalone Single-server deployments, edge nodes, development Standalone Installation Docker Containerized environments, quick evaluation Docker Installation Cloudera Manager CDP clusters with centralized management Cloudera Manager Installation

docs.title: Creating the Admin Account
docs.text:
On first launch, Query Pulse has no users. The first user to register automatically receives the ADMIN role.

  1. Open Query Pulse in your browser (http://<hostname>:8088)
  2. Click Register on the login page
  3. Enter a username, email, and password
  4. This first account becomes the admin

The admin username defaults to admin, but you can choose any username during registration. Only the first registered user gets the ADMIN role automatically.

Resetting the admin password (if locked out):

# Set this environment variable and restart the application
export QUERY_PULSE_ADMIN_PASSWORD_RESET=true

After restart, the admin password is cleared and you can log in and set a new one. Remember to unset the variable afterward.

docs.title: Creating Additional Users
docs.text:
Local authentication (default): Users self-register through the login page, or an admin can create accounts from Settings > User Management.

LDAP authentication: An admin must pre-create user accounts before users can log in. The local username must match the LDAP directory username. See LDAP Setup below.

OIDC authentication: Users are automatically provisioned on first SSO login (when QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_DYNAMIC_USER_CREATE=true). See OIDC Setup below.

docs.title: Authentication Setup
docs.text:
Query Pulse supports three authentication modes. Set the mode with:

export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=local    # local | ldap | oidc

Regardless of which mode you choose, the admin account always authenticates with local credentials. This ensures admin access even when LDAP or OIDC services are unavailable.

docs.title: Local Authentication (Default)
docs.text: No additional configuration needed. Users register and log in with a username and password stored in the application database.

docs.title: LDAP Authentication Setup
docs.text:
Delegates password verification to an external LDAP or Active Directory server.

Required parameters:

export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=ldap
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldap://ldap.example.com:389
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_DN="cn=serviceaccount,dc=example,dc=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_PASSWORD="service-password"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_BASE="ou=users,dc=example,dc=com"

Optional parameters:

Variable Default Description QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_FILTER (uid={0}) LDAP filter; {0} is replaced with the username QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USERNAME_ATTR uid Attribute containing the username QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_SSL false Use LDAPS (port 636) QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USE_STARTTLS false Upgrade to TLS on port 389 QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_SKIP_SSL_VERIFY false Skip certificate verification (testing only)

Active Directory example:

export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=ldap
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_URL=ldap://ad.corp.example.com:389
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_DN="CN=svc-querypulse,CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=example,DC=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_BIND_PASSWORD="service-password"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_BASE="CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=example,DC=com"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USER_SEARCH_FILTER="(sAMAccountName={0})"
export QUERY_PULSE_LDAP_USERNAME_ATTR=sAMAccountName

Important: Users must be pre-registered in Query Pulse before they can log in with LDAP. The local username must match the LDAP directory username. Register users via the admin UI or have them self-register first.

For the full reference, see LDAP Authentication.

docs.title: OIDC / SSO Setup
docs.text:
Enables Single Sign-On via an OpenID Connect identity provider (Keycloak, Okta, Azure AD, Google, or any OIDC-compliant provider).

Required parameters:

export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=oidc
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ISSUER_URI=https://keycloak.example.com/realms/myrealm
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=query-pulse
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret

Optional parameters:

Variable Default Description QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_SCOPES openid profile email groups OAuth2 scopes to request QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_USER_GROUP (empty) Require membership in this group; empty allows all QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ADMIN_GROUP (empty) Members of this group get the ADMIN role QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_DYNAMIC_USER_CREATE true Auto-create local accounts on first SSO login

Identity provider setup:

In your IdP, create a client (or application) with:

  • Redirect URI: https://<query-pulse-host>:8088/login/oauth2/code/oidc
  • Grant type: Authorization Code
  • Token claims: Ensure preferred_username, email, and groups (or roles) claims are included in the ID token

Example with Keycloak:

export QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE=oidc
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ISSUER_URI=https://keycloak.example.com/realms/myrealm
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=query-pulse
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET=<from-credentials-tab>
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_USER_GROUP=query-pulse-users
export QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ADMIN_GROUP=query-pulse-admins

When OIDC is enabled, the login page displays a Login with SSO button alongside the username/password form. The password form remains available for the admin account.

For the full reference including Okta and Azure AD examples, see OIDC / SSO Authentication.

docs.title: Truststore Configuration
docs.text: When connecting to endpoints secured with self-signed or internal CA certificates (Hive over SSL, LDAPS, OIDC providers with private certificates), Query Pulse needs to trust those certificates.

docs.title: Uploading Certificates via the UI
docs.text:
The simplest approach is to upload certificates through the application:

  1. Log in as any user (or admin for global certificates)
  2. Go to Settings > Certificates
  3. Click Upload Certificate
  4. Select a .crt, .cer, or .pem file and provide an alias
  5. The certificate is added to the application's truststore

Importing from an existing JKS truststore:

  1. Go to Settings > Certificates
  2. Click Import from Truststore
  3. Select your .jks file and enter the truststore password
  4. Choose which certificates to import

User-uploaded certificates are scoped to that user. Admin users can upload Global certificates that are available to all users.

docs.title: Configuring a System Truststore
docs.text:
To point the entire JVM at a truststore (for LDAPS, OIDC provider connections, etc.):

export QUERY_PULSE_TRUSTSTORE_LOCATION=/path/to/truststore.jks
export QUERY_PULSE_TRUSTSTORE_PASSWORD=changeit

This sets the JVM system properties javax.net.ssl.trustStore and javax.net.ssl.trustStorePassword, so all outbound SSL connections (LDAP, OIDC discovery, JDBC) will trust the certificates in this truststore.

For deployment-specific truststore examples, see the Standalone, Docker, or Cloudera Manager installation guides.

docs.title: Creating a Truststore
docs.text:
If you have a CA certificate or server certificate in PEM format, create a JKS truststore with:

keytool -importcert \
  -alias my-ca \
  -file ca-certificate.pem \
  -keystore truststore.jks \
  -storepass changeit \
  -noprompt

To add multiple certificates:

keytool -importcert -alias hive-ca -file hive-ca.pem -keystore truststore.jks -storepass changeit -noprompt
keytool -importcert -alias ldap-ca -file ldap-ca.pem -keystore truststore.jks -storepass changeit -noprompt
keytool -importcert -alias oidc-ca -file idp-ca.pem  -keystore truststore.jks -storepass changeit -noprompt

docs.title: Connecting to Your First Data Source
docs.text: Once Query Pulse is running and you have logged in:

docs.title: Step 1 — Install a JDBC Driver
docs.text:
1. Go to Settings > JDBC Drivers
  2. Select the tab for your engine (Hive, Trino, Impala)
  3. Click Install next to the driver matching your platform (e.g., CDP 7.3.1 Hive)
  4. Wait for the download to complete

docs.title: Step 2 — Create a Connection
docs.text:
1. Go to Settings > Connections
  2. Click Add Connection
  3. Provide:
  4. Connection Name — A descriptive label
  5. Platform Type — Must match the installed driver
  6. JDBC URL — Your endpoint URL

Example JDBC URLs:

# HiveServer2 with Kerberos
jdbc:hive2://hiveserver2.example.com:10000/default;principal=hive/_HOST@REALM.COM

# HiveServer2 with username/password
jdbc:hive2://hiveserver2.example.com:10000/default

# HiveServer2 via Knox Gateway (SSL)
jdbc:hive2://knox.example.com:8443/;ssl=true;transportMode=http;httpPath=gateway/cdp-proxy-api/hive

# Trino
jdbc:trino://trino.example.com:8443/hive/default?SSL=true

# HiveServer2 with ZooKeeper discovery (HA)
jdbc:hive2://zk1:2181,zk2:2181,zk3:2181/;serviceDiscoveryMode=zooKeeper;zooKeeperNamespace=hiveserver2
  1. Click Save & Test to verify connectivity

docs.title: Step 3 — Start Querying
docs.text:
1. Click Workspaces in the navigation bar and create a workspace
  2. Open or create a .sql file
  3. Select your connection from the connection dropdown
  4. Write a query and click Run

docs.title: Common Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Default Description QUERY_PULSE_SERVER_PORT 8088 HTTP/HTTPS port QUERY_PULSE_CONTEXT_PATH / URL context path QUERY_PULSE_HEAP_INIT 2048 Initial JVM heap (MB) QUERY_PULSE_HEAP_MAX 4096 Maximum JVM heap (MB) QUERY_PULSE_LOG_LEVEL INFO Log verbosity (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR) QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE local Authentication mode (local, ldap, oidc) QUERY_PULSE_ADMIN_PASSWORD_RESET false Reset admin password on startup QUERY_PULSE_SSL_ENABLED false Enable HTTPS for the application QUERY_PULSE_TLS_PROTOCOL TLSv1.2 TLS protocol version QUERY_PULSE_TRUSTSTORE_LOCATION (empty) Path to JKS truststore QUERY_PULSE_TRUSTSTORE_PASSWORD (empty) Truststore password

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• SQL Editor — Learn about the multi-dialect editor features
  • Code Assist — Configure intelligent autocomplete
  • Security & Certificates — Set up TLS/mTLS for the application
  • LDAP Authentication — Full LDAP configuration reference
  • OIDC / SSO Authentication — Full OIDC configuration reference
  • Administration — JVM tuning, RocksDB management, and more

docs.title: Real-Time Feedback
docs.text: Query Pulse provides real-time execution feedback for long-running queries, giving you visibility into query progress instead of waiting blindly for completion.

docs.title: Why Real-Time Feedback Matters
docs.text:
Traditional SQL tools show a spinning cursor while queries execute. You have no idea: - Is the query still running or stuck? - How much longer will it take? - Which stage is currently executing? - Are there any failures in progress?

Query Pulse solves this with streaming progress updates that show exactly what's happening during query execution.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Query Pulse uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) to stream progress updates from the database engine to your browser in real-time:

Browser ←──SSE Stream──← Backend ←──Progress API──← Database Engine

Progress updates arrive as events containing: - Current execution stage - Task completion counts - Elapsed time - Error information (if any)

docs.title: Hive Progress Tracking
docs.text:
Hive queries using the Tez execution engine provide detailed Beeline-style progress:

Stage Information: - Map stages processing input data - Reducer stages aggregating results - Vertex stages in the execution DAG

Task Counters per Stage:

Counter Description Total Number of tasks created Completed Tasks finished successfully Running Tasks currently executing Pending Tasks waiting to start Failed Tasks that encountered errors Killed Tasks terminated early

Stage Status Colors:

Status Color Meaning PENDING Gray Not started INITIALIZING Blue Setting up RUNNING Blue Actively executing COMPLETED Green Finished successfully FAILED Red Error occurred KILLED Orange Terminated

Execution Summary:

When hive.tez.exec.print.summary=true is configured, additional details are available after completion: - Query Execution Summary with CPU time and I/O statistics - Task Execution Summary with per-vertex metrics - Counter breakdowns (HIVE, FileSystem, Shuffle)

docs.title: Trino Progress Tracking
docs.text:
Trino provides comprehensive query statistics through its Query Stats API:

Query State:

State Description QUEUED Waiting in queue PLANNING Generating execution plan RUNNING Actively executing FINISHING Completing final operations FINISHED Completed successfully FAILED Error occurred

Task Metrics: - Total, Running, Completed, Failed task counts

Driver Metrics: - Total, Running, Queued, Blocked, Completed driver counts

Data Processing: - Physical input data size and row counts - Processed input data size and row counts

Timing Breakdown:

Metric Description Elapsed Time Total wall-clock time Execution Time Time spent executing Queued Time Time waiting in queue Analysis Time Query analysis duration Planning Time Distributed planning duration CPU Time Total CPU consumed Scheduled Time Total scheduled time Blocked Time Time waiting for resources

Memory Metrics: - Peak user memory reservation - Peak total memory reservation - Current memory reservation

docs.title: Summary View
docs.text:
A compact single-line display showing: - Progress bar with percentage - Current stage description - Elapsed time

Best for: Quick status checks, limited screen space

docs.title: Detailed View
docs.text:
Expanded display showing: - All stage information with task counters - Complete timing breakdown - Memory and resource metrics

Best for: Debugging, performance analysis, detailed monitoring

docs.title: Switching Views
docs.text: Set your preferred view in User Preferences: 1. Open Settings dropdown 2. Select Preferences 3. Choose Query Progress View: Summary or Detailed

docs.title: Execution Details Panel
docs.text: After query completion, the Execution Details panel provides comprehensive information:

docs.title: Hive Details
docs.text:
• Query ID: Hive-assigned identifier
  • DAG ID: Tez DAG identifier
  • Application ID: YARN application ID
  • Execution Time: Total duration
  • Status: Final query status
  • Execution Summary: Parsed summary tables and counters

docs.title: Trino Details
docs.text:
• Query Statistics Grid: Organized metrics by category
  • Operator Summaries: Per-operator execution statistics
  • Expandable Details: Click operators for detailed metrics
  • Spill Detection: Highlighted warnings for disk spills

docs.title: Canceling Queries
docs.text:
Long-running queries can be canceled:

  1. Click the "Cancel" button during execution
  2. The query is terminated on the server
  3. Progress display shows cancellation status

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text: Benefit Description Visibility Know exactly what's happening during execution Early Detection Spot problems before queries complete Performance Insight Identify slow stages and bottlenecks Time Estimation Progress percentage helps estimate completion Resource Monitoring Track memory and CPU usage Debugging Detailed metrics for troubleshooting

docs.title: Supported Operations
docs.text:
Real-time feedback is available for:

  • Single statement execution
  • Script execution (per-statement progress)
  • Explain operations
  • Validation operations

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Monitor Failed Tasks: High failure counts may indicate data or resource issues
  2. Watch Blocked Time: High blocked time suggests resource contention
  3. Track Memory: Approaching peak memory may cause spills
  4. Check Stage Balance: Uneven task counts may indicate data skew
  5. Use Detailed View: When debugging performance issues
  6. Use Summary View: For routine query monitoring

docs.title: Repository First
docs.text: Query Pulse takes a "Repository First" approach to workspace management. Every workspace is backed by a full Git repository, enabling version control, collaboration, and remote synchronization for all your SQL development work.

docs.title: Core Philosophy
docs.text:
Traditional SQL tools store queries as isolated files or database records. Query Pulse treats your SQL work as a proper software development project:

  • Version Control: Every change is tracked in Git history
  • Branching: Work on multiple features or experiments simultaneously
  • Collaboration: Push and pull from remote repositories
  • History: Full audit trail of all changes with commit messages
  • Recovery: Easily revert to any previous state

docs.title: Workspace Architecture
docs.text:
When you create a workspace, Query Pulse automatically initializes a Git repository:

~/.query-pulse/workspaces/
├── {userId}/
│   └── {workspaceId}/
│       └── repo/           # Git repository root
│           ├── .git/       # Git metadata
│           └── files/      # Your SQL files

Each workspace is isolated by user, ensuring privacy and security of your work.

docs.title: New Empty Workspace
docs.text:
1. Click "Create Workspace" in the workspace selector
  2. Enter a workspace name
  3. Click "Create"

A new Git repository is automatically initialized with a main branch.

docs.title: Clone from Remote Repository
docs.text:
1. Click "Create Workspace" in the workspace selector
  2. Enter a workspace name
  3. Provide the remote repository URL (HTTPS or SSH)
  4. Select stored Git credentials (if required)
  5. Optionally specify a branch to checkout
  6. Click "Create"

The clone operation runs asynchronously. The workspace status shows "Cloning" until complete.

docs.title: Status Tracking
docs.text:
The Git panel shows real-time repository status:

Indicator Meaning Clean No uncommitted changes Modified Files changed but not staged Staged Files ready to commit Untracked New files not yet added to Git Conflicting Merge conflicts need resolution

docs.title: File Status in Tree
docs.text:
The file tree displays Git status with visual indicators:

  • Green - New file (added)
  • Yellow - Modified file
  • Red - Deleted file
  • Gray - Untracked file

docs.title: Committing Changes
docs.text:
1. Make changes to your SQL files
  2. Open the Git panel (Changes tab)
  3. Stage files individually or use "Stage All"
  4. Enter a commit message describing your changes
  5. Click "Commit"

docs.title: Branch Management
docs.text:
Work on multiple development lines:

  • Create Branch: Start a new feature branch from any commit
  • Switch Branch: Checkout different branches to switch context
  • Merge Branch: Combine branches when work is complete
  • Delete Branch: Clean up merged branches

docs.title: History and Diffs
docs.text:
• Commit History: View all commits with messages, authors, and timestamps
  • File Diffs: See exactly what changed between versions
  • Commit Details: Inspect individual commits with full diff information

docs.title: Connecting to Remotes
docs.text:
After creating a workspace, add remote repositories:

  1. Open Git panel
  2. Go to Remotes section
  3. Click "Add Remote"
  4. Enter remote name (e.g., "origin") and URL
  5. Associate stored credentials for authentication

docs.title: Push and Pull
docs.text:
Push: Upload your local commits to the remote repository - Select the remote and branch - Click "Push" - Uses stored credentials for authentication

Pull: Download and merge remote changes - Select the remote - Click "Pull" - Automatically merges with your current branch

Fetch: Download remote changes without merging - Updates remote tracking branches - Review changes before merging

docs.title: Credential Management
docs.text:
Store credentials securely for remote operations:

Type Use Case SSH Key GitHub/GitLab SSH URLs Personal Access Token HTTPS with token auth Username & Password Basic HTTPS auth

Credentials are encrypted at rest and associated per-remote, allowing different credentials for different repositories.

docs.title: Stash Management
docs.text:
Temporarily save uncommitted work:

  • Create Stash: Save current changes without committing
  • Apply Stash: Restore stashed changes (keeps stash)
  • Pop Stash: Restore and remove stash
  • Drop Stash: Delete a stash

docs.title: Tag Support
docs.text:
Mark important versions:

  • Create Tag: Mark commits with version labels
  • Lightweight Tags: Simple pointers to commits
  • Annotated Tags: Tags with messages and metadata

docs.title: Conflict Resolution
docs.text:
When merges result in conflicts:

  1. Conflicting files are marked in the file tree
  2. Open the file to see conflict markers
  3. Edit to resolve conflicts
  4. Stage the resolved file
  5. Commit the merge

docs.title: Workspace Status
docs.text:
Workspaces track their operational state:

Status Description PENDING Initial creation state INITIALIZING Git repository being set up CLONING Remote clone in progress READY Workspace ready for use ERROR Operation failed (see status message)

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Commit Often: Make small, focused commits with clear messages
  2. Use Branches: Create branches for new features or experiments
  3. Write Good Messages: Describe why you made changes, not just what
  4. Push Regularly: Backup your work to remote repositories
  5. Review Before Commit: Use diffs to verify your changes
  6. Keep Main Clean: Merge completed, tested work to main branch

docs.title: Benefits of Repository First
docs.text:
• Never Lose Work: Git history preserves every change
  • Experiment Safely: Create branches for risky changes
  • Collaborate: Share work through remote repositories
  • Audit Trail: Know who changed what and when
  • Portable: Clone workspaces to any machine
  • Industry Standard: Leverage familiar Git workflows

docs.title: Global vs User-Scoped Settings
docs.text: Understanding the scope of each setting is important for proper configuration management:

docs.title: Global Settings (Admin Only)
docs.text:
These settings affect the entire application and all users:

Setting Description Restart Required JDBC Drivers Database driver installations No TLS/mTLS Application encryption Yes RocksDB Database configuration Varies JVM Settings Java runtime parameters Yes LLM Configuration AI provider settings Yes Encryption Keys Master key management No

docs.title: User-Scoped Settings
docs.text:
Each user maintains their own independent configuration:

Setting Description Shared Connections HiveServer2 connections No Kerberos Configs Authentication credentials No Git Credentials Repository access tokens No Certificates Personal SSL certificates No

docs.title: Hybrid Settings
docs.text:
Some settings support both global and user-specific configurations:

Setting Global User-Specific Certificates Admin can create system-wide certs Users can add personal certs

docs.title: Security & Certificates
docs.text: This section covers TLS configuration, certificate management, and encryption settings.

docs.title: TLS/mTLS Configuration
docs.text:
Scope: Global (Admin Only)

Configure application-level TLS encryption for secure communication.

docs.title: TLS Modes
docs.text: Mode Description One-Way TLS Server certificate only; clients verify server Mutual TLS (mTLS) Both server and client certificates required

docs.title: Server Certificate Configuration
docs.text:
Distinguished Name (DN) Fields: - Common Name (CN): Server hostname - Organization (O): Company name - Organizational Unit (OU): Department - Country (C): Two-letter country code - State (ST): State or province - Locality (L): City

Subject Alternative Names (SAN): - Additional hostnames or IP addresses - Critical for certificate validation

Certificate Parameters: - Validity: 30-3650 days - Key Size: 2048 or 4096 bits - Protocol: TLSv1.2 or TLSv1.3

docs.title: Client Certificate Management (mTLS)
docs.text: When mTLS is enabled: 1. Generate client certificates for authorized clients 2. Download PKCS12 file for client configuration 3. Track certificate status and expiration 4. Revoke certificates when needed

docs.title: Configuration Workflow
docs.text:
1. Enable TLS
  2. Generate or import server certificate
  3. Configure DN and SAN values
  4. Optionally enable mTLS
  5. Generate client certificates if needed
  6. Restart application to apply changes

docs.title: Certificates
docs.text:
Scope: Hybrid (User-Scoped + Global for Admin)

Manage X.509 certificates for SSL/TLS connections to external services.

docs.title: Certificate Sources
docs.text:
JDK Default Certificates: - Automatically available - Read-only, cannot be modified - Include common CA certificates

User Certificates: - Uploaded by individual users - User-scoped (not visible to other users) - Can be deleted by the owning user

Global Certificates (Admin Only): - Created by admin users - Available to all users - Marked with "Global" badge

docs.title: Certificate Upload
docs.text:
Individual Certificate Upload: - Supported formats: .crt, .cer, .pem - Specify alias for identification - Certificate details parsed automatically

JKS Truststore Import: - Import multiple certificates at once - Provide truststore password - Select certificates to import

docs.title: Certificate Information Displayed
docs.text:
• Alias (name)
  • Subject
  • Expiration date
  • File location
  • File size
  • Status badges (Global, Read-only)

docs.title: Truststore Management
docs.text:
The application maintains a truststore at: 

~/.query-pulse/security/truststore.jks

Operations available: - View truststore contents - Reset truststore (with backup) - Delete individual certificates

docs.title: Master Password & Encryption
docs.text:
Scope: Global (Admin Only)

The Master Password is the passphrase that protects the PKCS12 keystore containing Query Pulse's master encryption key. It is not the admin user login password. Every piece of sensitive data stored by Query Pulse -- connection passwords, Kerberos keytabs, Kerberos passwords, TLS passwords, and Git credentials -- is encrypted using keys derived from the master encryption key inside this keystore. If the master password is lost or changed without updating the configuration, all previously encrypted data becomes unrecoverable.

docs.title: Encryption Architecture
docs.text:
The master password unlocks a PKCS12 keystore file that holds a single AES-256 master encryption key (alias: master-encryption-key). That master key is never used directly for encrypting application data. Instead, purpose-specific keys are derived from it using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with 100,000 iterations, providing cryptographic domain separation.

All encryption uses AES-256-GCM (authenticated encryption) with a random 12-byte IV per operation and 128-bit GCM authentication tags. Additional Authenticated Data (AAD) ties each ciphertext to its owning entity (e.g., conn:<userId>:<connectionId>), preventing ciphertext from being moved between records.

                    +--------------------------+
                    |  QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_   |
                    |  PASSWORD (env var)      |
                    +-----------+--------------+
                                |
                                v
                    +-----------+--------------+
                    |  PKCS12 Keystore         |
                    |  (query-pulse-keys.p12)  |
                    |                          |
                    |  [master-encryption-key]  |
                    |  (AES-256)               |
                    +-----------+--------------+
                                |
                    PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256
                    (100,000 iterations)
                                |
          +----------+----------+----------+----------+
          |          |          |          |          |
          v          v          v          v          v
     connection  keytab    kerberos    tls      git
     passwords   encrypt   passwords  passwords creds
     (derived)   (derived) (derived)  (derived) (derived)
          |          |          |          |          |
          v          v          v          v          v
     AES-256-GCM  AES-256-GCM  ...      ...        ...
     + random IV  + random IV
     + AAD        + AAD

docs.title: What's Encrypted
docs.text: Data Type Purpose Key Used For Connection passwords connection-passwords JDBC/database connection credentials Kerberos keytabs keytab-encryption Binary keytab file encryption Kerberos passwords kerberos-passwords Kerberos principal passwords TLS passwords tls-passwords SSL/TLS keystore/truststore passwords Git credentials git-credentials Repository access tokens General general Default/fallback encryption

docs.title: Keystore File Location
docs.text:
Deployment Mode Default Path Standalone ~/.query-pulse/security/query-pulse-keys.p12 Docker /data/query-pulse/security/query-pulse-keys.p12 (inside container) Cloudera Manager (CSD) /var/lib/query-pulse/security/query-pulse-security.p12

The path is configurable via:

  • Spring property: query-pulse.security.keystore.path
  • Environment variable: QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PATH

docs.title: Configuration Properties
docs.text:
Property Environment Variable Default Description query-pulse.security.keystore.path QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PATH ${query-pulse.working-directory-base}/security/query-pulse-keys.p12 Path to the PKCS12 keystore file query-pulse.security.keystore.password QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD query-pulse-dev-keystore-password Password protecting the keystore

WARNING: The default password query-pulse-dev-keystore-password is intended for development only. Always set a strong, unique password for production deployments.

docs.title: First Run Behavior
docs.text:
On first startup, if no keystore file exists at the configured path:

  1. The parent directory is created if it doesn't exist.
  2. A new PKCS12 keystore is created.
  3. A random AES-256 master key is generated and stored in the keystore under the alias master-encryption-key.
  4. The keystore is saved and file permissions are restricted to owner read/write only.
  5. The master key is loaded into memory and the service reports initialized = true.

On subsequent startups, the existing keystore is loaded and the master key is extracted using the configured password.

docs.title: Password Resolution Order
docs.text:
When the application starts, the keystore password is resolved in this order:

  1. query-pulse.security.keystore.password (from environment variable QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD or Spring config)
  2. Hardcoded development default: query-pulse-dev-keystore-password -- a warning is logged

docs.title: Passing the Master Password by Deployment Mode
Standalone Mode (query-pulse.sh)
docs.text:
Set the environment variable before starting, or configure it in query-pulse-env-local.sh:

export QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD="your-strong-password"
./query-pulse.sh start

The startup script reads QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD and passes it as a Spring Boot command-line override:

--query-pulse.security.keystore.password=${KEYSTORE_PASSWORD}

The environment template file (query-pulse-env.sh) documents this variable:

# SECURITY/ENCRYPTION SETTINGS
# Keystore for encrypting sensitive data (passwords, credentials)
# export QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PATH=/var/lib/query-pulse/security/query-pulse-security.p12
# export QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD=password

docs.title: Docker Mode (docker-compose.yml)
docs.text:
Pass the password via environment variable before running docker-compose, or set it in a .env file:

# Shell
export QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD="your-strong-password"
docker-compose up -d

# Or in .env file
QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD=your-strong-password

The docker-compose.yml forwards it to the container:

environment:
  - QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD=${QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD:-}

Inside the container, the application's application.yaml resolves it via Spring's ${QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD:...} placeholder.

docs.title: Cloudera Manager (CSD) Mode
docs.text:
The CSD service.sdl defines two parameters on the Query Pulse Server role:

CSD Parameter Label Type Default query_pulse_keystore_path Query Pulse Keystore Path path (localDataFile) /var/lib/query-pulse/security/query-pulse-security.p12 query_pulse_keystore_password Query Pulse Keystore Password password (empty)

These are configured in the Cloudera Manager UI under the Query Pulse Server role configuration. The SDL maps them to environment variables:

"QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PATH": "${query_pulse_keystore_path}",
"QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD": "${query_pulse_keystore_password}"

The control.sh script picks them up and passes them as Spring Boot application arguments:

APP_ARGS+=("--query-pulse.security.keystore.path=${KEYSTORE_PATH}")
if [ -n "$KEYSTORE_PASSWORD" ]; then
    APP_ARGS+=("--query-pulse.security.keystore.password=${KEYSTORE_PASSWORD}")
fi

The password is sanitized in configuration logs:

KEYSTORE_PASSWORD:     [SET - 24 chars]

docs.title: Changing the Master Password
docs.text: The master password can be changed through the Admin UI or the REST API. This changes the password that protects the keystore; it does not rotate the master encryption key inside it (that is a separate operation).

docs.title: Via the Admin UI
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the Master Password panel in the Admin section.
  2. Enter the current password.
  3. Enter a new password (minimum 12 characters).
  4. Confirm the new password.
  5. Click Change Password.

On success:

  • The old keystore is backed up with a datetime suffix (e.g., query-pulse-keys-2026-02-11T14-30-45-123456.p12).
  • A new keystore is created with the same keys but the new password.
  • The UI displays the backup path and a warning about updating configuration.

docs.title: Via the REST API
docs.text:
POST /api/v1/admin/security/keystore/change-password

Request body: 

{
  "currentPassword": "old-password",
  "newPassword": "new-strong-password-at-least-12-chars"
}

Success response: 

{
  "status": "SUCCESS",
  "message": "Keystore password changed successfully",
  "backupPath": "/path/to/query-pulse-keys-2026-02-11T14-30-45-123456.p12",
  "entriesTransferred": 1,
  "warning": "You must update the keystore password in your configuration ..."
}

This endpoint requires the ADMIN role.

docs.title: What Happens During Password Change
docs.text:
1. The current password is validated by attempting to load the existing keystore.
  2. A backup of the old keystore is created with a timestamp suffix.
  3. A new keystore is created and all entries are transferred from the old keystore using the new password.
  4. The new keystore is written to a temporary file first, then atomically swapped.
  5. Restrictive file permissions (owner-only read/write) are set on the new keystore.
  6. The in-memory keystore reference is updated.

docs.title: Post-Change Requirements
docs.text:
After changing the master password, you must:

  1. Update your deployment configuration with the new password:
    • Standalone: Update QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD in your environment or query-pulse-env-local.sh.
    • Docker: Update QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD in your .env file or docker-compose environment.
    • Cloudera Manager: Update the "Query Pulse Keystore Password" in the CM configuration UI.
  2. Restart the application for the new password to take effect on next startup.

If the configuration is not updated, the application will fail to start on next restart with error KEYSTORE_004: Invalid keystore password.

docs.title: Master Key Rotation (Separate from Password Change)
docs.text: Key rotation replaces the master encryption key inside the keystore and re-encrypts all stored data. This is distinct from changing the keystore password. Key rotation is available via the REST API only.

docs.title: When to Rotate
docs.text:
• Suspected key compromise
  • Compliance requirements (periodic rotation policy)
  • Personnel changes (key holders departing)

docs.title: Via the REST API
docs.text:
POST /api/v1/admin/security/rotate-key?confirm=true

Without confirm=true, the endpoint returns a confirmation prompt listing affected data types and a recommendation to create a database backup before proceeding.

docs.title: Rotation Process
docs.text:
1. Collect: All encrypted data is decrypted with the current key (connections, Kerberos configs, keytabs).
  2. Rotate: A new AES-256 master key is generated. The old key is backed up in the keystore with a timestamped alias.
  3. Re-encrypt: All data is re-encrypted with the new key and saved back to the database.
  4. Clear cache: The derived key cache is cleared so new purpose-specific keys are derived from the new master key.

Important: Key rotation should be performed during a maintenance window. If rotation fails mid-process, check logs and consider restoring from backup.

docs.title: Encryption Validation
docs.text:
Validate that all encrypted data can still be decrypted via the REST API:

GET /api/v1/admin/security/validate

Returns success/failure counts for connections, Kerberos configs, and keytabs. Use this to verify encryption health after upgrades, key rotation, or suspected issues.

docs.title: Health Monitoring
docs.text:
Monitor the overall encryption system health via the REST API:

GET /api/v1/admin/security/health

Key Management Status:

  • HEALTHY: Master key loaded, keystore accessible
  • NOT_INITIALIZED: Key management service failed to start

Data Encryption Status:

  • HEALTHY: All stored data decrypts successfully
  • ISSUES_FOUND: One or more items failed to decrypt

Additional Endpoints:

Endpoint Method Description /api/v1/admin/security/status GET Keystore initialization status, path, derived key cache size /api/v1/admin/security/keystore GET Non-sensitive keystore info (path, algorithm, type)

All security management endpoints require the ADMIN role.

docs.title: Error Codes
docs.text:
The KeystoreException class provides structured error codes and recovery instructions:

Error Code Description Recovery KEYSTORE_001 Keystore file not found Restart to create a new keystore; previously encrypted data is unrecoverable KEYSTORE_002 Permission denied Check file permissions on the keystore directory KEYSTORE_003 Keystore corrupted Delete corrupted keystore and restart; previously encrypted data is unrecoverable KEYSTORE_004 Wrong password Verify QUERY_PULSE_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD matches the password used when the keystore was created KEYSTORE_005 Key not found Keystore was likely recreated; affected data must be re-entered KEYSTORE_006 Key generation failed Check system entropy and Java security configuration KEYSTORE_007 Decryption failed Keys have changed; affected data must be re-entered KEYSTORE_008 Encryption failed Check system resources and Java security configuration KEYSTORE_009 Initialization failed Check logs and ensure the keystore directory is writable

docs.title: Main Navigation (All Users)

  • Queries: Execute and manage database queries

docs.title: SQL Editor
docs.text: Query Pulse provides a powerful SQL editing environment designed for data engineers and analysts working with LakeHouse platforms. The editor supports multiple SQL dialects, real-time query progress, and comprehensive result management.

docs.title: Editor Overview
docs.text: The SQL Editor is the primary workspace for writing and executing SQL queries. It combines a modern code editor with database connectivity, variable substitution, and rich result visualization.

docs.title: Multi-Tab Sessions
docs.text:
Work with multiple database connections simultaneously:

  • Each tab maintains its own session and connection context
  • Switch between connections without losing previous results
  • Execute queries in parallel across different databases
  • Sessions preserve state when switching between file tabs

docs.title: SQL Dialect Support
docs.text:
The editor supports multiple SQL dialects with dialect-specific features:

Dialect Features Hive Full Hive SQL, EXPLAIN variants (Visual, CBO, Dependency, Authorization, Vectorization) Trino Trino SQL, EXPLAIN with TYPE and FORMAT options Impala Coming soon

docs.title: Syntax Highlighting
docs.text: The editor provides real-time syntax highlighting for SQL keywords, strings, numbers, and comments. Highlighting adapts automatically to your chosen theme (Day/Night).

docs.title: Code Formatting
docs.text:
Format your SQL for better readability:

  1. Format Selection: Select text and format only that portion
  2. Format All: Format the entire query

Two formatters are available:

  • T-SQL Formatter: Fast, client-side formatting (always available)
  • Calcite Formatter: Advanced formatting via the backend (preferred for DML)

Access formatting via the toolbar or right-click context menu.

docs.title: Code Assist
docs.text:
The editor provides intelligent autocompletion for:

  • SQL keywords and functions
  • Table and column names from your schema
  • Database/schema names

For detailed information on Code Assist features, see the Code Assist Guide.

docs.title: Find and Replace
docs.text:
• Find: Ctrl+F (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+F (Mac)
  • Replace: Ctrl+R (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+R (Mac)

Features: - Case-sensitive search option - Highlight all matches - Navigate between matches with arrow buttons

docs.title: Execution Modes
docs.text: Mode Shortcut (Win/Linux) Shortcut (Mac) Description Run Statement Ctrl+Enter Cmd+Enter Execute the statement at cursor position Run Script Ctrl+Shift+Enter Cmd+Shift+Enter Execute all statements sequentially Run from Cursor Ctrl+Alt+Enter Cmd+Alt+Enter Execute from cursor to end of file

docs.title: Statement Detection
docs.text:
The editor automatically detects statement boundaries:

  • Statements are separated by semicolons (;)
  • Comments and quoted strings are properly handled
  • Leading comments can serve as statement labels

docs.title: Cancel Query
docs.text: Click the Cancel button to stop a running query. The editor shows "Cancelling..." status during the cancellation process.

docs.title: Variable Substitution
docs.text: Use variables to parameterize your queries and avoid hardcoding values.

docs.title: Defining Variables
docs.text:
Variables can be defined at two levels:

  1. Workspace Variables: Shared across all files in the workspace
  2. File Variables: Override or extend workspace variables for a specific file

docs.title: Using Variables
docs.text:
Reference variables in your SQL using the ${variable_name} syntax:

SELECT *
FROM ${database}.${table}
WHERE date = '${run_date}'
LIMIT ${max_rows}

docs.title: Missing Variables
docs.text:
If your query contains undefined variables:

  1. Execution is paused
  2. A dialog prompts you to define missing variables
  3. Once defined, execution continues

docs.title: File Variables Panel
docs.text:
The right sidebar includes a Variables panel where you can:

  • View all available variables (workspace + file)
  • Add file-level variables
  • Override workspace variables
  • Delete file-level overrides

docs.title: Result Tabs
docs.text:
After execution, results appear in tabs below the editor:

Tab Content Results Query output as a data table Statements History of executed statements with timing Details Query metadata, statistics, and operator info Progress Live execution progress (during query run)

docs.title: Table View
docs.text:
The default result display with:

  • Column headers with data types
  • Sortable columns
  • Row numbers
  • Scrollable content

docs.title: Filtering Results
docs.text:
Filter displayed rows using the filter box:

Pattern Matches text Rows containing "text" (case-insensitive) ^prefix Rows starting with "prefix" suffix$ Rows ending with "suffix" ^exact$ Exact match only /regex/ Regular expression pattern

The filter status shows: Showing X of Y rows

docs.title: Pagination
docs.text:
Navigate large result sets:

  • Page Size: Default 25 rows per page (configurable in preferences)
  • Navigation: Previous/Next buttons and direct page input
  • Range Display: Shows current row range (e.g., "1-25 of 1,000")

docs.title: Raw View
docs.text:
Switch to Raw View for tab-separated output, useful for:

  • Copy/paste into spreadsheets
  • Text processing
  • Quick data inspection

docs.title: Export Options
docs.text:
Export results in multiple formats:

Format Description CSV Comma-separated values TSV Tab-separated values Markdown Markdown table format Excel XLSX spreadsheet

Exports respect the current filter, exporting only visible rows.

docs.title: Real-Time Tracking
docs.text:
During query execution, view live progress:

  • Overall completion percentage
  • Stage-by-stage breakdown
  • Elapsed time (mm:ss)
  • Task counters: Running, Completed, Pending, Failed

docs.title: View Modes
docs.text:
• Summary: High-level progress overview
  • Detailed: Per-stage task breakdown

docs.title: Platform-Specific Metrics
docs.text:
Hive/Tez: - DAG progress - Vertex status - Task completion counts

Trino: - Query state (QUEUED, RUNNING, FINISHED) - CPU and memory usage - Data processing statistics - Execution time breakdown

docs.title: Explain Plans
docs.text: Visualize query execution plans without running the query.

docs.title: Hive EXPLAIN Options
docs.text: Option Description Visual Explain Graphical DAG representation Visual Cost Cost-based optimization plan Explain Dependency Table and column dependencies (JSON) Explain Authorization Required permissions Explain Vectorization Vectorized execution plan

docs.title: Trino EXPLAIN Options
docs.text:
Type Description LOGICAL Logical execution plan DISTRIBUTED Distributed plan with stages IO I/O operations plan VALIDATE Syntax validation only

Output formats: TEXT, GRAPHVIZ (visual), JSON

docs.title: Viewing Plans
docs.text:
• Visual plans open in the DOT Graph Editor
  • JSON/text output opens in a new file tab
  • Plans are versioned for comparison

docs.title: Query Execution
docs.text: Action Windows/Linux Mac Run Statement Ctrl+Enter Cmd+Enter Run Script Ctrl+Shift+Enter Cmd+Shift+Enter Run from Cursor Ctrl+Alt+Enter Cmd+Alt+Enter

docs.title: File Operations
docs.text: Action Windows/Linux Mac Save Ctrl+S Cmd+S Find Ctrl+F Cmd+F Replace Ctrl+R Cmd+R

docs.title: Editor
docs.text: Action Windows/Linux Mac Undo Ctrl+Z Cmd+Z Redo Ctrl+Y Cmd+Shift+Z Select All Ctrl+A Cmd+A

docs.title: Theme Support
docs.text:
The SQL Editor adapts to your theme preference:

  • Day Theme: Light background with dark text (GitHub Light)
  • Night Theme: Dark background with light text (GitHub Dark)
  • Auto: Follows your system preference

Change themes in Settings > Preferences > Theme.

docs.title: Other Editors
docs.text: Query Pulse includes specialized editors for different file types:

docs.title: Code Editor (YAML, JSON, XML)
docs.text:
For configuration and data files:

  • Syntax highlighting per language
  • Save/dirty state tracking
  • Git integration

docs.title: DOT Graph Editor (.dot files)
docs.text:
For visualizing execution plans and graphs:

  • Source View: Edit DOT code directly
  • Split View: Editor and preview side-by-side
  • Graph View: Full-screen rendered graph
  • Zoom controls (in, out, fit to view)
  • Live preview updates

docs.title: Markdown Editor (.md files)
docs.text:
For documentation:

  • Source View: Edit Markdown
  • Split View: Editor and preview
  • Preview View: Rendered Markdown
  • Syntax highlighting in code blocks
  • Live preview

docs.title: Efficient Query Development
docs.text:
1. Use variables for values that change between runs
  2. Format your SQL before committing to version control
  3. Use Run Statement to test individual queries in a script
  4. Check EXPLAIN before running expensive queries

docs.title: Working with Large Results
docs.text:
1. Filter early in your SQL with WHERE clauses
  2. Use LIMIT during development
  3. Export filtered results to reduce file size
  4. Use pagination to navigate without loading all rows

docs.title: Connection Management
docs.text:
1. Create connections for each database you work with
  2. Set workspace defaults for commonly used connections
  3. Override at file level when needed for specific scripts

docs.title: Trino Features
docs.text: Query Pulse provides comprehensive support for Trino with real-time query statistics, configurable explain plans, SQL validation, and detailed operator-level analytics.

docs.title: Real-Time Query Statistics
docs.text: When executing queries against Trino connections, Query Pulse displays live query statistics from the Trino Query Stats API.

docs.title: Progress Display
docs.text:
The progress display shows real-time updates during query execution:

  • Query State: Current execution state (QUEUED, PLANNING, RUNNING, FINISHING, FINISHED, FAILED)
  • Progress Percentage: 0-100% completion indicator
  • Elapsed Time: Total time since query started

docs.title: Query State Indicators
docs.text:
States are color-coded for quick identification:

State Color Description QUEUED Gray Query waiting in queue PLANNING Blue Query plan being generated RUNNING Blue Query actively executing FINISHING Blue Query completing final operations FINISHED Green Query completed successfully FAILED Red Query encountered an error

docs.title: Task Metrics
docs.text:
Real-time task execution statistics:

Metric Description Total Tasks Number of tasks created Running Tasks Currently executing tasks Completed Tasks Successfully finished tasks Failed Tasks Tasks that encountered errors

docs.title: Driver Metrics
docs.text:
Detailed driver execution information:

Metric Description Total Drivers Total drivers created Running Drivers Currently executing drivers Queued Drivers Drivers waiting to execute Blocked Drivers Drivers waiting on resources Completed Drivers Successfully finished drivers

docs.title: Data Processing Metrics
docs.text:
Live data throughput statistics:

Metric Description Physical Input Data Bytes read from storage Physical Input Positions Rows read from storage Processed Input Data Bytes processed after filtering Processed Input Positions Rows processed after filtering

docs.title: Timing Metrics
docs.text:
Comprehensive timing breakdown:

Metric Description Elapsed Time Total wall-clock time Execution Time Time spent executing Queued Time Time spent in queue Analysis Time Time for query analysis Planning Time Time for distributed planning CPU Time Total CPU time consumed Scheduled Time Total scheduled time Blocked Time Time waiting for resources

docs.title: Memory Metrics
docs.text:
Memory consumption tracking:

Metric Description Peak User Memory Maximum user memory used Peak Total Memory Maximum total memory used Current Total Memory Current memory reservation

docs.title: Explain Plan Features
docs.text: Query Pulse provides a configurable explain interface for Trino with multiple type and format options.

docs.title: Using Trino EXPLAIN
docs.text: How to Use: 1. Write or select a query in the SQL editor 2. Click the Trino EXPLAIN button (tree icon) 3. A configuration popup appears 4. Select TYPE and FORMAT options 5. Click "Execute"

docs.title: Explain Types
docs.text:
Type Description LOGICAL Shows the logical execution plan DISTRIBUTED Shows the distributed execution plan across nodes

LOGICAL Plan: Displays the query plan at the logical level, showing: - Operator tree structure - Predicate expressions - Column projections - Join conditions

DISTRIBUTED Plan: Shows how the plan is distributed across Trino workers: - Stage boundaries - Data exchange operations - Partition strategies - Remote exchanges

docs.title: Output Formats
docs.text:
Format Description File Extension TEXT Human-readable text format .txt GRAPHVIZ DOT graph format for visualization .dot JSON Structured JSON format .json

TEXT Format: Best for quick review and sharing: 

- Output[columnA, columnB]
    - RemoteExchange[GATHER]
        - TableScan[table, ...]

GRAPHVIZ Format: Best for visual analysis: - Creates a DOT file that can be rendered as a graph - Shows operator relationships visually - Useful for complex query plans

JSON Format: Best for programmatic analysis: - Structured data format - Contains all plan details - Can be processed by external tools

docs.title: File Output
docs.text:
Explain operations create files in the workspace:

Combination File Pattern LOGICAL + TEXT {filename}_trino_explain_logical_{n}.txt LOGICAL + GRAPHVIZ {filename}_trino_explain_logical_{n}.dot LOGICAL + JSON {filename}_trino_explain_logical_{n}.json DISTRIBUTED + TEXT {filename}_trino_explain_distributed_{n}.txt DISTRIBUTED + GRAPHVIZ {filename}_trino_explain_distributed_{n}.dot DISTRIBUTED + JSON {filename}_trino_explain_distributed_{n}.json

Files are automatically opened in new editor tabs for viewing.

docs.title: SQL Validation
docs.text: Trino provides a fast validation feature to check SQL syntax and semantics without executing the query.

docs.title: Using Validate SQL
docs.text: How to Use: 1. Write or select a query in the SQL editor 2. Click the Validate SQL button (checkmark icon) 3. Results appear in the results panel

docs.title: Validation Results
docs.text:
Valid Query: - Green panel displays "Valid" - Confirms query syntax is correct - Confirms all referenced objects exist - Confirms user has required permissions

Invalid Query: - Red panel displays "Invalid" - Query has syntax errors, or - Referenced objects don't exist, or - User lacks required permissions

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Rapid Development: Check syntax while writing queries
  • Pre-Execution Check: Validate before running expensive queries
  • Permission Testing: Verify access without consuming resources
  • CI/CD Integration: Validate SQL files before deployment

Command Executed: EXPLAIN (TYPE VALIDATE) <query>

docs.title: Execution Details
docs.text: After query completion, detailed execution information is available in the Execution Details panel.

docs.title: Query Statistics Grid
docs.text:
Statistics are organized into logical groups:

Task Progress: - Total, Completed, Running, Failed task counts

Driver Progress: - Total, Completed, Running, Queued, Blocked driver counts

Data Processing: - Physical and processed input sizes - Row counts at each stage

Timing: - Complete timing breakdown by category

Memory: - Peak and current memory usage

docs.title: Operator Summaries
docs.text:
Detailed per-operator execution statistics table:

Column Description Stage Stage.Pipeline.Operator ID Operator Operator type name Drivers Number of drivers for operator Input Input data size and rows Output Output data size and rows CPU Time CPU time consumed Peak Memory Maximum memory used

docs.title: Expandable Operator Details
docs.text:
Click any operator row to see additional metrics:

Input Metrics: - Physical input data size - Physical input positions (rows) - Processed input data size - Read time - Add input time

Output Metrics: - Output data size - Output positions (rows) - Get output time

Resource Usage: - Peak user memory - Peak total memory - Blocked time - Spilled data size (highlighted if > 0)

docs.title: Spill Detection
docs.text: When an operator spills data to disk, it's highlighted as a performance concern: - Spilled data size shown in warning color - Indicates memory pressure during execution - Suggests potential for query optimization

docs.title: Supported Query Types
docs.text:
Explain and validation features are available for the following statement types:

  • SELECT
  • INSERT
  • UPDATE
  • DELETE
  • MERGE

DDL statements (CREATE, ALTER, DROP) are not supported for explain operations.

docs.title: Variable Substitution
docs.text:
All explain and validation operations support workspace and file variables:

-- Using variables in queries
SELECT * FROM ${catalog}.${schema}.${table} WHERE date = '${run_date}'

Variables are substituted before the command is executed. If undefined variables are detected, a dialog prompts for values.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use LOGICAL EXPLAIN to understand the query structure and optimizer decisions
  2. Use DISTRIBUTED EXPLAIN when investigating parallelism and data distribution issues
  3. Use GRAPHVIZ format for complex queries to visualize the plan
  4. Use JSON format when you need to process the plan programmatically
  5. Use Validate SQL frequently during query development for rapid feedback
  6. Monitor Blocked Time in execution details - high values indicate resource contention
  7. Watch for Spilled Data - indicates memory pressure and potential optimization opportunities
  8. Review Operator Summaries to identify bottleneck operators in slow queries

docs.title: User Menu
docs.text: Located in the top-right corner, showing: - Username and email - Profile link - Sign Out option

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Query Pulse supports three authentication modes, configured via the QUERY_PULSE_AUTH_TYPE environment variable:

Mode Description User Provisioning local (default) Users register and authenticate with a username and password stored in the application database Manual registration ldap Users authenticate against an LDAP or Active Directory server Users must be pre-registered locally oidc Users authenticate via an OpenID Connect Identity Provider (SSO) Automatic on first login (configurable)

All modes use JWT (JSON Web Token) based stateless authentication for API requests:

  • Login: Users authenticate via their configured method
  • Token Refresh: Access tokens can be refreshed without re-authentication
  • Session Management: Tokens are stored client-side; logout clears local storage

docs.title: Local Authentication
docs.text: The default mode. Users register with a username, email, and password. Passwords are stored as bcrypt hashes in the local database.

docs.title: LDAP Authentication
docs.text: Delegates credential verification to an external LDAP or Active Directory server. Users must be pre-registered in Query Pulse with a username matching their directory entry. See LDAP Authentication for setup instructions.

docs.title: OIDC / SSO Authentication
docs.text: Enables Single Sign-On via an OpenID Connect provider (Keycloak, Okta, Azure AD, etc.). Users are automatically provisioned on first login. See OIDC / SSO Authentication for setup instructions.

docs.title: Admin Account
docs.text: Regardless of the authentication mode, the admin account (default username: admin) always authenticates using local credentials. This ensures admin access is maintained even when external authentication services are unavailable.

docs.title: Admin vs Non-Admin Users
docs.text:
The application distinguishes between two user types:

Capability Admin User Regular User Manage own connections Yes Yes Manage own Kerberos configs Yes Yes Manage own Git credentials Yes Yes Upload certificates Yes Yes View system status Yes Yes Manage JDBC drivers Yes No Configure TLS/mTLS Yes No Manage RocksDB Yes No Configure JVM settings Yes No Configure LLM providers Yes No Rotate encryption keys Yes No Manage other users Yes No Create global certificates Yes No

Admin Role Assignment:

  • Local mode: The first registered user automatically receives the ADMIN role. Additional admins can be promoted by existing admins.
  • LDAP mode: Admin role is managed manually through the Query Pulse user management interface.
  • OIDC mode: Admin role can be automatically assigned based on IdP group membership via QUERY_PULSE_OIDC_ADMIN_GROUP.

docs.title: User Registration
docs.text:
New users can self-register through the application:

  1. Navigate to the login page
  2. Click "Register"
  3. Provide username, email, and password
  4. Account is created and immediately active

Admin users can also: - View all registered users - Enable/disable user accounts - Reset user passwords - View user statistics (workspaces, connections, credentials count)
